requests.raw_requirement: (Sample) Incident tickets
requests.request_text: Ticket or relevant documentation that shows that incidents are logged, tracked, and communicated to affected parties, by management until resolved, initially and at least weekly or as required. Include all column headers or fields typically filled with information about the incident.

requests.raw_requirement: (Sample) Configurations validating mobile device patching status
requests.request_text: Mobile device patch status demonstrating that the devices received the most up-to-date approved patches. Patches should include operating systems.

requests.raw_requirement: (Sample) Visitor logs
requests.request_text: For facilities, datacenters, and secure areas provide the visitor log that demonstrates logging of visitor access to secure areas. Secure areas means areas which contain, process, store, transmit or otherwise house customer data and protected information based on the sample selection provided or the last 90 days.

requests.raw_requirement: (Sample) Terminated personnel facility access removal tickets
requests.request_text: Termination ticket for terminated personnel should show the date when the individual's access authorization was revoked and the date when physical access was removed.

requests.raw_requirement: (Sample) Antivirus agent configurations
requests.request_text:
Configurations implementing central management of antivirus agent configurations. Managed configured settings include:
- Scan frequency setting, showing whether scans occur on boot and on a subsequent periodic basis (or indicate continuous as applicable for Crowdstrike, network-based malware detection, etc)
- Definition update setting 
- Types of hosts this is applied to (e.g. if Linux or Mac hosts are excluded, please show this or label as Windows only)
- Dashboard or central management configuration showing number of active hosts and the total number of offline hosts
- Settings that prohibit users from disabling the agent
- Settings to mitigate phishing attacks.
- Removable media malware analysis

requests.raw_requirement: (Sample) Cardholder data access logs
requests.request_text: Configurations or supporting records demonstrating access to cardholder data are logged and attributable to individual users. Supporting records may include logs demonstrating user attribution and recorded dates and times of sensitive data access.

requests.raw_requirement: (Sample) Configurations enforcing session timeouts or automatic disconnections for mobile devices
requests.request_text: Configuration of mobile devices enforced through MDM for session timeout and automatic disconnections. Configuration should align with the company's policy for timeouts for mobile devices.

requests.raw_requirement: (Sample) Change tickets relevant to selected mobile device (e.g. laptop, phone)
requests.request_text: Supporting record demonstrating the submission, approval, and tracking of changes to applicable mobile devices.

requests.raw_requirement: (Sample) Change tickets tracking changes to infrastructure
requests.request_text: Change tickets for infrastructure changes, showing documentation of impact, approval, functionality testing, and rollback procedures.

requests.raw_requirement: (Sample) Current employee security awareness training records
requests.request_text: Ensure the security awareness training completion artifact includes the name of the current employee and the date when the training was completed.

requests.raw_requirement: (Sample) Patch deployment to production systems
requests.request_text: Logs from automated patch management solutions or change tickets demonstrating adherence to organizations' patch management policy.

requests.raw_requirement: (Sample) Network traffic flow (inbound and outbound) rules
requests.request_text: Traffic flow rules that are in place to enforce controls over inbound and outbound traffic to and from the internet and supporting policies from the component that applies filters to inbound and outbound traffic from firewalls.

requests.raw_requirement: (Sample) Secure coding training records
requests.request_text: Ensure the secure coding training completion artifact includes the name of the software developer and the date when the training was completed.

requests.raw_requirement: (Sample) Key custodian agreements
requests.request_text: Sample of completed key custodian forms and designated backup(s) authorized to have physical access to keyed materials and components.

requests.raw_requirement: (Sample) Mobile or employee owned device firewall configurations
requests.request_text: Configurations of mobile and/or employee owned devices demonstrating that device firewall is enabled and unmodifiable by the employee when the device is outside of the approved environment, commonly the corporate and/or trusted network.

requests.raw_requirement: (Sample) Servers implement one primary function
requests.request_text: Configuration evidence showing that for the servers within the environment, each of them has only implemented one primary function in order to prevent a security incident due to differing security levels on the same servers. This equally applies to both virtual and physical server system components.

requests.raw_requirement: (Sample) Vendor default changed
requests.request_text: Configuration evidence showing that built-in vendor default accounts, credentials, and other security settings (i.e., SNMP, wireless, etc.) have been changed to non-default values.

requests.raw_requirement: (Sample) Custom and bespoke application changes
requests.request_text: Sample of documented custom and/or bespoke application change tickets.

requests.raw_requirement: (Sample) Common password configurations
requests.request_text:
Configuration evidence demonstrating that common security password configurations have been implemented to include:
- Lock out
- Lock out duration
- Idle timeout
- Complexity
- Rotation
- Password History

requests.raw_requirement: (Sample) Encryption used for open and public network locations with cardholder data
requests.request_text: Sample of strong encryption for authentication and transmission for locations where cardholder data is transmitted and/or received over open, public networks.

requests.raw_requirement: High-level network diagrams
requests.request_text:
High-level network diagrams include the following required components: 
- All physical locations in the report need to be represented clearly on the diagram(s) (ex. retail store, data center, call center, corporate, etc.)
- Diagram(s) needs to clearly show in-scope and out-of-scope segments.  This is best achieved with shaded boxes around each area with a key to explain colors/shading.
- All inbound and outbound connections to the CDE must be represented in the diagram(s). Communication outside the CDE needs to show protections for card data inbound/outbound.
- Network segmentation used to reduce the scope should be included. The boundary of in-scope and out-of-scope networks, and the networking devices providing the segmentation should be clearly labeled.
- All segments need to be clearly labeled. Labels must match any segmentation narratives provided.
- Wireless segments should be specifically called out in the diagram(s) with appropriate icons. 
- Representative icons on the diagrams must exist for all security devices related to controls, any system in the payment flow, and any other  critical/key systems providing services to the CDE.
- Diagram(s) should be dated, including a reviewed by date that is within the current assessment year.
- Virtualization technologies should be clearly identified on the diagram(s) separately indicating the hypervisor, related management hosts, and then the actual virtual/guest systems.
- A key to explain the diagram icons and coloring/shading.
 
Detailed network diagrams include the following required components: 
The segmentation boundaries and inbound/outbound connections are more detailed than the high level diagram(s): For these diagrams: 
- Clearly illustrate CDE versus non-CDE. 
- Clearly illustrate all connections to the CDE from areas outside the CDE.
- Clearly illustrate all known wireless devices both in and out of the CDE.
- Clearly illustrate all connections to third parties, card brands, or other business units. 
- Clearly illustrate all physical locations in the report on compliance. 
- Include how CHD is protected (protocol and encryption) for inbound/outbound connections over public networks.
- All included segments must be labeled. Labels used need to be the same across all diagrams in the entire report for reconciliation purposes. 
- Provide a key to explain the diagram icons and coloring/shading. 
- Ensure that diagrams are dated, including a reviewed by date that is within the current assessment year.
(ROC|ES, 1.2.3.a, 1.2.3.b, 1.3.3, 1.4.1.a, 1.4.4.a, 6.5.3.b, 11.5.1.a)

requests.raw_requirement: Cardholder data (CHD) flow diagrams
requests.request_text:
Diagrams should include the following required components:
-Be in the form of a flow chart. 
-Cover all acceptance channels/processing flows of cardholder data such as: 
  -Capture, authorization, settlement, chargeback, etc. 
  -Other flows of any card data as applicable to the assessed environment. 
  -Include which CHD is involved (PAN, expiration, name, card validation codes/values, track data, PIN, etc.). 
  -Any and all storage and protections applied to the stored data (ex. encryption algorithm, truncation method, etc.). 
  -Any payment applications used (software used for transaction authorization and/or settlement). 
  -Include protections on the communication channel (protocol/encryption) for communication across open/public networks or untrusted networks. 
  -Include any wireless segments/transmission. 
  -Include any interaction by personnel with CHD. 
  -Include a "reviewed" date on the diagram, which must be within a year of this assessment. 
  -Include a key to explain the diagram icons and coloring/shading.
  -If CHD is not stored, processed, or transmitted, a data flow diagram is not necessary.
(ROC|ES, 1.2.4.a, 1.2.4.b, 1.4.4.a)

requests.raw_requirement: Inventory of all network segments
requests.request_text:
Provide the completed inventory spreadsheet for all network segments within the environment. Include in-scope, out-of-scope and wireless (if applicable). (Network Segment Empty Template.xlsx in the Project Files section of CE)

Note that network segments should include corporate and guest networks, as well as both wired and wireless segments. 
(ROC|ES, 12.5.1)

requests.raw_requirement: Inventory of payment capture devices
requests.request_text:
Provide completed inventory spreadsheet for all in-scope payment capture devices. (Payment Capture Device Empty Template.xlsx in the Project Files section of CE)

Note that payment capture devices could include (but are not limited to):
- Point of Interaction (POI) devices 
- Point of Sale (POS) terminals with swipe readers  
-portable devices / tablets with swipe readers
(ROC|ES, 9.5.1.1.a, 12.5.1)

requests.raw_requirement: Inventory of in-scope servers
requests.request_text:
Provide of all completed inventory spreadsheet for all in-scope servers. (Server Empty Template.xlsx in the Project Files section of CE)

Note that servers could include (but are not limited to):
 - Database servers (for stored CHD) 
 - Systems supporting: 
 - Payment Application - application server(s), web server(s), storage server(s) 
 - Authentication - Active Directory 
 - non-AD authentication solutions (i.e.. network device management, SSO solutions, multifactor authentication, etc.) 
 - Patching 
 - Logging / monitoring solution, management and storage 
 - File integrity solution, management and storage  
 - Anti-malware
(ROC|ES, 12.5.1)

requests.raw_requirement: Inventory of in-scope workstations
requests.request_text:
Provide completed inventory spreadsheet for all in-scope workstations. (Workstation Empty Template.xlsx in the Project Files section of CE)

Note that workstations could include both PCs as well as laptops.  
The CDE inventory is required to be consistent with all other information provided.
(ROC|ES, 12.5.1)

requests.raw_requirement: Inventory of all network equipment
requests.request_text:
Provide completed inventory spreadsheet for all in-scope network devices and appliances. (Network Equipment Empty Template.xlsx in the Project Files section of CE)

Note that network devices and appliances could include (but are not limited to): 
 - Firewalls, routers and switches (layer 2 & 3) 
 - IDS IPS appliance (if / as applicable) 
 - Load balancer (if / as applicable) 
 - NTP appliance (if / as applicable) 
 - Wireless controllers, wireless access points (WAPs)  
(ROC|11.2.2, 12.5.1)

requests.raw_requirement: Inventory of all applications, including bespoke and custom software
requests.request_text:
Provide completed inventory spreadsheet for all in-scope payment applications and security tools. (Application Empty Template.xlsx in the Project Files section of CE)

Note that applications and solutions may include (but are not limited to): 
- Payment application (third party, internally developed)
- Bespoke or custom applications
- Authentication solutions (Active Directory, or non-AD based) 
- Network device management 
- SSO solutions 
- Multifactor authentication
- Security management solutions 
- Patching 
- Logging / monitoring solution (SIEM) 
- File integrity solution (FIM) 
- anti-malware 
- Vulnerability scanning tools 
- Software / code scanning tools
(ROC|ES, 6.3.2.a, 6.3.2.b, 12.5.1)

requests.raw_requirement: Inventory of all data repositories
requests.request_text:
Provide completed inventory spreadsheet for all in-scope data repositories. (Data Repository Empty Template.xlsx in the Project Files section of CE)

Note that data repositories may include:
- Databases with stored PAN
- Call recordings
- Backup tapes
- Paper records
- Removable media
- Discarded hardware
(ROC|ES, 12.5.1)

requests.raw_requirement: Inventory of all virtualization technologies
requests.request_text:
Provide completed inventory spreadsheet for all in-scope virtual technologies. (Virtual Technology Empty Template.xlsx in the Project Files section of CE)

Note that virtual technologies could include (but are not limited to): 
- Hypervisors 
- Supporting infrastructure for Citrix, other VDI solutions

(ROC|ES, 12.5.1)

requests.raw_requirement: Inventory of all physical locations
requests.request_text:
Provide completed inventory spreadsheet for all in-scope physical locations. (Physical Location Empty Template.xlsx in the Project Files section of CE)

Note that physical locations could include (but are not limited to): 
- Corporate HQ, remote office locations 
- Data center(s) 
- Hot / warm / cold failover DR sites 
- Retail / restaurant locations 
- Call center(s) 
- Hospital / clinic locations 
(ROC|ES, 12.5.1)

requests.raw_requirement: Inventory of all third party service providers
requests.request_text:
Provide completed inventory spreadsheet for all in-scope service providers. (Service Provider Empty Template.xlsx in the Project Files section of CE)

Service providers are: 
Business entity that is not a payment brand, who is directly involved in the processing, storage, or transmission of cardholder data on behalf of another entity. This also includes companies that provide services that control or could impact the security of cardholder data. Examples include managed service providers that provide managed firewalls, IDS, and other services as well as hosting providers and other entities. If an entity provides a service that involves only the provision of public network access such as a telecommunications company providing just the communication link the entity would not be considered a service provider for that service (although they may be considered a service provider for other services). 
Note that service providers could include (but are not limited to):
- Merchant banks  
- Processors / gateways 
- Software as a Service (SaaS) 
- Infrastructure as a Service (IaaS) 
- Platform as a Service (PaaS) 
- Other managed security service 
- SOC (Security Operations Center) 
- NOC (Network Operations Center) 
- Staff augmentation  network / systems management 
- Fraud check 
(ROC|ES, 12.5.1, 12.8.1.b)

requests.raw_requirement: Last four quarters of external vulnerability ASV scans and applicable rescans
requests.request_text:
As external networks are at greater risk of compromise, quarterly external vulnerability scanning must be performed by a PCI SSC Approved Scanning Vendor (ASV). 
- A robust scanning program ensures that scans are performed and vulnerabilities addressed in a timely manner. 
- Quarterly scans should be performed every 90 days on all in-scope components and networks.  Rescans must occur if any vulnerabilities are identified and remediation actions are taken.  If vulnerabilities are not remediated, and rescans are not completed, a business justification must be documented and a compensating control worksheet may be required. 
- Note that any change control documentation - change tickets, etc. - associated with vulnerability scan remediation validation should be presented to your QSA for evidence review.  
- Note that (4) quarterly external ASV scans are expected to have been performed in the past 12-month period.
(ROC|11.3.2.a, 11.3.2.b, 11.3.2.c, 11.3.2.1.a, 11.3.2.1.a, 11.3.2.1.b)

requests.raw_requirement: Current Attestation of Compliance documentation
requests.request_text:
It is important to monitor the compliance status of service providers and other third parties with which the entity shares cardholder data and could affect the security of cardholder data. 
Note that an AOC might not be available for each listed service provider.  Should that be the case, consult your QSA to confirm what additional evidence or testing might be necessary such that you can complete  your compliance assessment.
(ROC|12.8.4.b)

requests.raw_requirement: Lists of roles and all personnel with access to the CDE, systems, devices and applications, and CHD
requests.request_text:
As part of a PCI engagement, the Coalfire QSA is required to obtain and review a sample of roles and a sample of personnel with access - general and privileged - to the cardholder environment. 
From the full listings of roles and of all applicable personnel that have access in the cardholder environment; the QSA will then select a subset for inclusion within the assessment report. 
Note that the QSA may sample based upon personnel name, system user IDs, or role types.  Refer to your QSA's direction for evidence provision when requested.
(ROC|6.5.4, 7.1.2, 7.2.6, )

requests.raw_requirement: Non-listed encryption solutions in use within the PCI environment.
requests.request_text:
Provide each of the following document(s): 
 - Current non-listed Encryption Solution Assessment (NESA) by a P2PE QSA, which identifies any applicable PCI DSS scope reduction. 
 - Current whitepaper by a P2PE QSA, which identifies any applicable PCI DSS scope reduction. 
 - Instruction Manual 
 - Any PCI SP AOCs associated with the non-listed encryption solution.
(ROC|ES)

requests.raw_requirement: Contact information for reporting documents.
requests.request_text:
Provide the contact information for the business specific point of contact that will be noted in the PCI DSS Report on Compliance, Attestation of Compliance, or SAQ documentation. If a different individual will sign the documents, provide name, title, and email address for the signer.
(ROC|ES)

requests.raw_requirement: Description of the business
requests.request_text:
Business Description: Describe the nature of your business -  what kind of work your organization does, etc.

Description of the nature of your business (what kind of work you do), includes: 
 - whether the business is a merchant or service provider and at which level (Level 1, 2, 3, 4) 
 - If you are a service provider, what services are provided?  
 - Are you a card issuer?   
 - industry you operate in  retail, healthcare, service provider, hospitality (food / beverage, hotels, resorts), etc.  
 - types of customers you service,  
 - lines of business (ecommerce, call center; remote / virtual / physical brick-n-mortar, etc.) 
 - operating locations  retail stores / restaurant locations, hospital / clinics, remote, third party 
 - include international locations if / as relevant 
 - number of employees who handle CHD (cardholder data)  front end operations, back end administrative functions (finance, audit, etc.) 
 - number of IT employees who support the CDE (cardholder data environment) and / or handle CHD directly network / systems administrators, software developers, technical support, security team, etc.  
 - major IT contract / service providers (who provide service that supports / impacts the CDE) 
 - merchant banks, processors / gateways, fraud check 
 - Software as a Service (SaaS), Infrastructure as a Service (IaaS), Platform as a Service (PaaS) 
 - SOC (Security Operations Center), NOC (Network Operations Center) 
 - Staff augmentation  network / systems management, desktop support, software development
(ROC|ES)

requests.raw_requirement: Internal semi-annual scope reviews
requests.request_text:
PCI DSS scope is documented and confirmed by the entity at least once every 12 months and upon significant change to the in-scope environment. At a minimum, the scoping validation includes: 
-Identifying all data flows for the various payment stages (for example, authorization, capture settlement, chargebacks, and refunds) and acceptance channels (for example, card-present, card-not-present, and e-commerce). 
-Updating all data-flow diagrams per Requirement 1.2.4. 
-Identifying all locations where account data is stored, processed, and transmitted, including but not limited to: 1) any locations outside of the currently defined CDE, 2) applications that process CHD, 3) transmissions between systems and networks, and 4) file backups. 
-Identifying all system components in the CDE, connected to the CDE, or that could impact security of the CDE. 
-Identifying all segmentation controls in use and the environment(s) from which the CDE is segmented, including justification for environments being out of scope. 
-Identifying all connections from third-party entities with access to the CDE. 
-Confirming that all identified data flows, account data, system components, segmentation controls, and connections from third parties with access to the CDE are included in scope.
(ROC|ES, 12.5.2.a, 12.5.2.b, 12.5.2.1.a, 12.5.2.1.b)

requests.raw_requirement: Network segmentation used to reduce the scope of the assessment
requests.request_text:
Guidance from the ROC reporting template: 
-Explain which in scope environment/segment is segmented from which out of scope environment/segment, at the network level. 
Technologies used for segmentation:
-Firewall (explain how it is used for segmentation) 
-VLAN (explain how it is used for segmentation) 
-Switch (explain how it is used for segmentation) 

Supporting processes: 
-Change management (explain how it is used for segmentation) 
-Log monitoring and incident response (explain how it is used for segmentation) 
-Penetration testing and port scanning (explain how it is used for segmentation) 
-Firewall ruleset review (explain how it is used for segmentation)
(ROC|ES)

requests.raw_requirement: Configuration standards and hardening practices for wireless devices.
requests.request_text:
Provide formal internal configuration standards documentation / hardening practices for wireless devices.
Configuration Standard documents (policy/process, or other formal documentation) must include the following: 
 - That all vendor defaults (including default passwords on operating systems, software providing security services, application and system accounts, POS terminals, Simple Network Management Protocol (SNMP) community strings, etc.) are changed before a system is installed on the network. 
 - List what vendor defaults are changed 
 - That unnecessary default accounts (including accounts used by operating systems, security software, applications, systems, POS terminals, SNMP, etc.) are removed or disabled before a system is installed on the network. 
 - List what vendor defaults are removed 
 - Identify industry-accepted hardening standard(s) that the system configuration standards is consistent with.  
 - List industry-accepted hardening standard(s) 
 - That system configuration standards are updated as new vulnerability issues are identified.  
 - Indicate that this is done (must be stated in policy/process) 
 - That system configuration standards are applied when new systems are configured, and  verified as being in place before a system is installed on the network  
 - Indicate that this is done (must be stated in policy/process) 
 - That all vendor-supplied defaults are changed, and unnecessary default accounts are eliminated 
 - Indicate that this is done (must be stated in policy/process) 
 - That only one primary function per server is implemented (to prevent functions that require different security levels from co-existing on the same server) 
 - The demonstrated primary function should reconcile with the CDE inventory provided during the pre-assessment phase 
 - That only necessary services, protocols, daemons, etc., are enabled as required for the function of the system; 
 - List enabled necessary services, protocols, daemons, etc. 
 - Implementing additional security features for any required services, protocols or daemons that are considered to be insecure 
 - List additional security features for any insecure services, protocols or daemons, etc. that are required 
 - Configuring system security parameters to prevent misuse 
 - List implemented security parameters to prevent misuse of systems 
 - Removing all unnecessary functionality, such as scripts, drivers, features, subsystems, file systems, and unnecessary web servers 
 - List removed default functionality (scripts, drivers, features, subsystems, file systems, and unnecessary web services) 
 - That common security parameter settings are applied  
 - List applied common security parameter settings 
 - That enabled functions are documented  
 - The enabled functions should support what is indicated in the CDE inventory provided during the pre-assessment phase
(ROC|1.2.1, 2.2.1, 1.2.5.a, 1.2.6.a,  4.2.1.2)

requests.raw_requirement: Policies and procedures for change control
requests.request_text:
Provide formal internal change control process documentation that discusses how the organization handles changes within the technical environment.  
Documentation on change control processes must include content which addresses: 
-that development/test environments are separate from production environments with access control in place to enforce separation. 
-There is a separation of duties between personnel assigned to the development/test environments and those assigned to the production environment. 
-Production data (live PANs) are not used for testing or development. 
-Test data and accounts are removed before a production system becomes active.
-Detailed change-control procedures specific to implementing security patches and software modifications and includes at least the following: 
-Documentation of impact. 
-Documented change approval by authorized parties. 
-Functionality testing to verify that the change does not adversely impact the security of the system. 
-Backout procedures. 

For network specific changes - network connections and device configuration changes - a formal process must exist that documents the testing and approval by authorized parties. 
All network connections within an environment must be reviewed, analyzed, and approved prior to being implemented within a Cardholder Data Environment (CDE).  The document being requested must specifically require the organization to formally approve and track the approvals when making changes that affect network connections. 
Changes to networking equipment, and specifically routers and firewalls must always be tested prior to being implemented within a production environment.  The document(s) being requested should specifically require the organization to test all changes to routers and firewalls prior to implementation in production.  Note that while there may be relevant content overlap between change control, patching and software development,  there are specific requirements for each.  Reach out to your QSA for clarification if needed, and / or reference the requirements in the PCI DSS.
(ROC|1.2.2.a, 6.5.1.a, 6.5.2, 6.5.4.a)

requests.raw_requirement: Network security control configuration reviews
requests.request_text:
Provide evidence of the internal review of the rulesets / configuration of in-scope network security controls.
There should be (2) documented results, with associated evidence, spaced (6) months apart.  Evidence should show time / date of the effort so that the timeline of activity completion can be validated.  
Network security control configuration reviews should be conducted on a regular basis.  For PCI purposes, a regular basis is no less than every six months.  While performing a review, sufficient documentation should be created that shows at a minimum: 
 - What was reviewed 
 - The outcome of each review (e.g., keep, remove, update, etc.) 
 - Personnel that participated in the review 
 - The formal approver for the review 

A formal policy should be maintained within the organization that requires that staff perform these reviews.  
This document must be uploaded to support this requirement.
(ROC|1.2.7.a, 1.2.7.b)

requests.raw_requirement: Policies and procedures for the protection of CDE-connected portable devices
requests.request_text:
Provide formal internal documentation for how the company manages the secure use of portable devices (such as  laptops, tablets, computer-on-wheels, etc) used to access the CDE. If n/a, provide documentation prohibiting the use of such devices.
Documentation on change control processes must include content which addresses: 
-that personal firewall software or equivalent functionality is required for all portable computing devices (including company and/or employee-owned) that connect to the Internet when outside the network, (for example, laptops used by employees), and which are also used to access the CDE. 
-the specific configuration settings for personal firewall or equivalent functionality (should be defined). 
-that the personal firewall or equivalent functionality is configured to actively run. 
-that the personal firewall or equivalent functionality is configured to not be alterable by users of the portable computing devices. 

When it is necessary to allow portable employee-owned technologies to connect to the Cardholder Data Environment (CDE), and have the ability to connect to the Internet while not within the security confines of the corporate environment, specific security requirements must be applied to those mobile and/or personal devices.  To ensure that the security measures are implemented, they must be detailed within a formal policy.  The formal policy must include the following, at a minimum: 
-Personal firewall software is required for all mobile and/or employee-owned devices that connect to the Internet when outside the network, and which are also used to access the network. 
-Specific configuration settings are defined for personal firewall software. 
-Personal firewall software is configured to actively run. 
-Personal firewall software is configured to not be alterable by users of mobile and/or employee-owned devices.
(ROC|1.5.1.a)

requests.raw_requirement: Vendor configuration documentation for all in-scope systems
requests.request_text:
PCI requires that organizations implement systems, devices, applications and technical solutions in accordance with vendor documentation and / or industry standards, to ensure that all are optimized and operating correctly, including configuration, authentication, maintenance and updates.  
-Wireless networks must be configured with strong encryption for:-Access / authentication over wireless networks 
-Transmission of data over wireless networks 

As a part of the process, vendor documentation is required to be retained and utilized when configuring productions systems. Common vendor documentation includes administration guides, user guides, installation quick starts, and manuals. 
Note that multiple vendor documents may be provided, pending different services within an single enterprise level vendor solution.   Where multiple solutions are in use, multiple vendor documents are required.
If websites / URLs are appropriate, provide the entire URL path (versus a general website, and the QSA has to dig for the correct documentation).  Where a URL is provided, ensure that an organizational login is not required to retrieve the indicated vendor documentation. 
Specifically for Requirement 3, organizations must demonstrate adoption of secure mechanisms for remotely accessing systems and devices in the cardholder environment, commonly referred to as non-console administrative access.  This access is required to be implemented based on industry best practices to enforce strong encryption (strength and cipher), authentication, and transmission.
Specifically for Requirement 7.1, organizations must confirm what technology product(s) they are using to accomplish access control management for all systems, devices and applications within the CDE. Unless defined by a custom in-house product, the organization is required to provide vendor documentation for the access control mechanisms in use.

Documentation must include content which addresses: 
-The assignment of a unique ID to all users before allowing them to access system components or cardholder data. 
-The process to control addition, deletion, and modification of user IDs, credentials, and other identifier objects. 
-That access is immediately revoked for any terminated users. 
-That inactive user accounts are removed / disabled at least every 90 days. 
-The management of IDs used by vendors to access, support, or maintain system components via remote access as follows: 
-Enabled only during the time period needed and disabled when not in use. 
-Monitored when in use. 
-Limit repeated access attempts by locking out the user ID after not more than six attempts. 
-Set the lockout duration to a minimum of 30 minutes or until an administrator enables the user ID. 
-If a session has been idle for more than 15 minutes, require the user to re-authenticate to re-activate the terminal or session. 
-users must be authenticated using a unique ID and additional authentication for access to the cardholder data environment. 
-how the organization handles requests by users for modifying (changing, unlocking) authentication credentials, including that if a user requests a reset of an authentication credential by a non-face-to-face method, the users identity is verified before the authentication credential is modified. 
-how the organization handles password management of their users, including that: 
-First-time passwords/passphrases must be set to a unique value for each user. 
-First-time passwords/passphrases must be changed after the first use. 
-Reset passwords/passphrases must be set to a unique value for each user. 
-Reset passwords/passphrases must be changed after the first use. 
-how the organization communicates (distribution of a policy, etc.) and advises personnel to manage their individual credentials, including: 
-Guidance on selecting strong authentication credentials. 
-Guidance for how users should protect their authentication credentials. 
-Instructions for users not to reuse previously used passwords. 
-That users should change passwords if there is any suspicion the password could be compromised. 
-that use of group and shared IDs and/or passwords or other authentication methods are explicitly prohibited. 

If other authentication mechanisms are used (for example, physical or logical security tokens, smart cards, certificates, etc.) documentation must include: 
-Authentication mechanisms are assigned to an individual account and not shared among multiple accounts.
-Physical and/or logical controls are defined to ensure only the intended account can use that mechanism to gain access.
-In order to ensure that the organization can track all access to Cardholder Data (CHD) back to specific individuals, each user with access must have a unique account.
(ROC|1.4.2, 1.4.3, 2.2.2.b, 2.2.7.d, 2.3.1.b, 2.3.1.c, 3.3.2, 3.5.1.2.b, 4.2.2.b, 5.2.2, 7.3.1, 7.3.2, 7.3.3, 8.3.2.a, 8.5.1.a, 11.5.1.c)

requests.raw_requirement: Data retention policies, procedures, and schedules
requests.request_text:
Provide formal internal documentation that discusses the secure storage and handling of CHD, including retention practices.
A formal data retention policy identifies what data needs to be retained, and where that data resides, so it can be securely destroyed or deleted as soon as it is no longer needed. 
 The only cardholder data that may be stored after authorization is the primary account number or PAN (rendered unreadable), expiration date, cardholder name, and service code.
Understanding where cardholder data is located is necessary so it can be properly retained or disposed of when no longer needed. In order to define appropriate retention requirements, an entity first needs to understand their own business needs as well as any legal or regulatory obligations that apply to their industry, and/or to the type of data being retained. Documentation is required to address each of the four items below, to include: 
 - Limiting data storage amount and retention time to that which is required for legal, regulatory, and/or business requirements for data retention. 
 - Specific requirements for retention of cardholder data.  
 - Processes for secure deletion of cardholder data when no longer needed for legal, regulatory, or business reasons.  
 - A quarterly process for identifying and securely deleting stored cardholder data that exceeds defined retention requirements.
(ROC|3.2.1.a, 3.5.1.b, 3.5.1.b, 3.5.1.c)

requests.raw_requirement: Secure storage and handling of sensitive authentication data
requests.request_text:
The storage of Sensitive Authentication Data (SAD) post-authorization is strictly prohibited; however, there are business models that exist within the payment card industry that are required to maintain this data.

Documentation must include content which addresses: 
-Documented business justification for the storage of sensitive authentication data, if applicable. 
-Defined processes of how the organization will ensure that sensitive authentication data is not retained after authorization.  
-Defined process of securely deleting SAD and that the data is unrecoverable.  
-Ensure that when SAD is deemed required for business that it is adequately documented and justified.  If sensitive authentication data is stored, the business justification must be formally documented.

Storage of SAD pre-authorization is permitted, but it must be encrypted and securely deleted after authorization occurs.  Securely deleting the data must be performed to render it completely unrecoverable. A secure deletion mechanism must be adopted, documented and proper destruction procedures must be adequately communicated. 
If there is no approved business justification for storing SAD, this must be formally stated and the prohibition of stored SAD must be communicated within an organization.
(ROC|3.3.1.a, 3.3.1.b, 3.3.2, 3.3.3.a)

requests.raw_requirement: Masking of PAN and restriction of access to display full PAN.
requests.request_text:
Limiting access to the Primary Account Number (PAN) is a tenet of PCI security.  Ensuring that the organization is appropriately regulating who can access PANs is the first step in regulating access.  Ensure that documentation includes the following at a minimum: 
-A list of roles that need access to displays of full PAN is documented, together with a legitimate business need for each role to have such access. 
-PAN must be masked when displayed such that only personnel with a legitimate business need can see the full PAN. 
-All other roles not specifically authorized to see the full PAN must only see masked PANs. 

Note that the first six and last four digits are the maximum number of digits to be displayed when CHD is numerically displayed.
(ROC|3.4.1.a)

requests.raw_requirement: PAN is rendered unreadable anywhere it is stored
requests.request_text:
Security mechanisms must be in place to secure Cardholder Data (CHD).  The protection mechanisms must be adequately secured and configured to ensure appropriate operation.
Organization is required to provide documentation to confirm that PAN is only rendered using any of the approved methods below, to include: 
-One-way hashes based on strong cryptography 
-Truncation 
-Index tokens and pads, with the pads being securely stored  
-Strong cryptography, with associated key-management processes and procedures 

Note that supporting vendor documentation could also be leveraged here.
(ROC|3.5.1.a, 3.5.1.1.a,  3.5.1.2.a)

requests.raw_requirement: Data encryption policies and procedures
requests.request_text:
Documentation on key management must include content which addresses: 
 - the protection against disclosure and misuse of keys used for encryption of stored cardholder data. 
 - secure storage of the cryptographic keys used to encrypt/decrypt CHD and includes at least the following: 
 - Access to keys is restricted to the fewest number of custodians necessary. 
 - key-encrypting keys are at least as strong as the data-encrypting keys they protect. 
 - key-encrypting keys are stored separately from data-encrypting keys. 
 - Keys are stored securely in the fewest possible locations and forms as well as how keys must only exist in one (or more) of the following forms at all times: 
 - Encrypted with a key-encrypting key that is at least as strong as the data-encrypting key, and that is stored separately from the data-encrypting key. 
 - Within a secure cryptographic device (such as a hardware (host) security module (HSM) or PTS-approved point-of-interaction device). 
 - As key components or key shares, in accordance with an industry-accepted method.
(ROC|3.7.4, 3.7.5, 3.7.7, 3.7.8.a, 3.5.1.1.b, 3.5.1.1.c, 3.5.1.1.d)

requests.raw_requirement: Cryptography architecture documentation about key management
requests.request_text:
Service Provider organizations are required to maintain encryption key-management architecture documentation to more clearly define keys and key component controls and storage. Service providers are required to maintain documentation to minimally include each of the below items: 
 - Details of all algorithms, protocols, and keys used for the protection of cardholder data,  including key strength and expiry date 
 - Description of the key usage for each key  
 - Inventory of any hardware security modules (HSM)s and other secure cryptographic devices (SCD)s used for key management 

Part of providing services related to PCI is to ensure that customers are adequately educated about their responsibilities related to CHD and more specifically key management and protection.  Ensure that there is customer documentation created and disseminated that educates customers about key security, and includes the following at a minimum: 
 - secure transmission of keys  
 - secure storage of keys, and how to perform key ring updates 

If encryption keys are shared with customers ensure that there is customer documentation created and disseminated that educates customers about key security, and includes the following at a minimum: 
-secure transmission of keys 
-secure storage of keys, and how to perform key ring updates 

(ROC|3.6.1.1, 3.7.9)

requests.raw_requirement: Encryption key management and handling procedures
requests.request_text:
Documentation on key management must include content which addresses: 
-how to generate strong keys 
-how to securely distribute keys 
-how to securely store keys 
-the key cryptoperiod(s) for each key type - data encryption key [DEK] and key encryption keys [KEK] 
-how to complete a key change / rotation at the end of the defined cryptoperiod(s) (for example, after a defined period of time has passed and/or after a certain amount of cipher-text has been produced by a given key), as defined by the associated application vendor or key owner, and based on industry best practices and guidelines (for example, NIST Special Publication 800-57). 
-how to retire / replace a key when the integrity of the key has been weakened, or a key has been suspected of compromise 
-how any keys retained after retiring or replacing are not used for encryption operations 
-processes to prevent unauthorized substitution of keys. 

Ensure that key-management procedures are sufficiently addressing the security risk around cryptographic keys by developing and using appropriate cryptoperiods based on industry best practices and guidelines, such as NIST Special Publication 800-57. Ensure that documentation includes the factors used when defining cryptoperiods.  At a minimum, the calculation factors should include: 
-Sensitivity of the data  
-The risk of key compromise 
-The cost of new key generation
(ROC|3.6.1.a, 3.7.1.a,  3.7.2.a, 3.7.3.a, 3.6.4.a, 3.7.4.a, 3.7.4.b 3.7.5.a, 3.7.6.a, 3.7.7.a, 3.7.8.a, 3.6.1.2.a, 3.6.1.3)

requests.raw_requirement: Manual clear-text cryptographic key-management operations
requests.request_text:
Documentation on manual clear-text cryptographic key-management operations must include content which addresses the use of: 
 - Split knowledge of keys, such that key components are under the control of at least two people who only have knowledge of their own key components
 - Dual control of keys, such that at least two people are required to perform any key-management operations and no one person has access to the authentication materials of another.
(ROC|3.7.6.a)

requests.raw_requirement: Key custodian policies and procedures
requests.request_text:
Provide formal internal key-management documentation that addresses the responsibilities of the key custodians, including that formal acknowledgement of their responsibilities is required.
Documentation on key management must include content which addresses the processes (and associated physical or electronic documentation) for key custodians to acknowledge that they understand and accept their key-custodian responsibilities 

The role of key-custodian with an organization must be regulated in a manner that ensures those creating and managing keys are aware of the proper methodologies to use, as well as the security implications of their management efforts. Documentation must require custodians:
-be properly informed of their responsibilities 
-acknowledge their responsibilities in writing
(ROC|3.7.8.b)

requests.raw_requirement: Policies and procedures for the transmission of account data over open-public networks
requests.request_text:
Provide formal internal documentation of configuration standards for the security protocols and the strong cryptography in use to protect transmitted CHD over public and open networks.

Organizations are required to implement strong encryption (strength, cipher, and algorithm) for the transport of cardholder information across public and wireless networks (defined below). 
 Open, public networks include but are not limited to: 
 - The Internet 
 - Wireless technologies, including 802-11 and Bluetooth 
 - Cellular technologies, such as, Global System for Mobile communications (GSM), Code division multiple access (CDMA) 
 - General Packet Radio Service (GPRS) 
 - Satellite communications 

Documentation on the security of transmitted CHD must include content which addresses: 
 - the acceptance of only trusted keys and/or certificates, and how. 
 - the protocol in use must only support secure versions and configurations (and insecure versions or configurations are not supported). 
 - implementation of proper encryption strength per the encryption methodology in use (i.e. must be strong cryptography, commensurate with the type of encryption).  

The security of transmitted CHD must include content includes at least the following: 
 - identification of the security protocols permitted for transmitting CHD 
 - identification of the strong cryptography required for securing transmitted CHD 
 - identification of the vendor recommendation / industry standard for the documented encryption strength
(ROC|2.3.1.a, 2.3.2, 4.2.1.a, 4.2.1.1)

requests.raw_requirement: Unprotected CHD is not to be sent via end-user messaging technologies
requests.request_text:
E-mail, instant messaging, Text message/SMS, and chat can be easily intercepted by packet-sniffing during delivery across internal and public networks. Do not utilize these messaging tools to send PAN unless they are configured to provide strong encryption.
Additionally, if an entity requests PAN via end-user messaging technologies, the entity should provide a tool/method/documented procedure to protect the PAN using strong cryptography or render PAN unreadable before transmission.
Note that this can be N/A if end-user technologies are in use by your organization.  Please advise your QSA if your organization is using end-user messaging technologies, as additional testing will be required, including process review and observations of outbound data transmissions.
(ROC|4.2.2.a)

requests.raw_requirement: Anti-malware solution detects, removes, and protects CHD systems from malware
requests.request_text:
PCI requires that organizations implement anti-malware solutions in accordance with vendor documentation to ensure they are optimized and operating correctly, including configuration, maintenance and updates (for endpoints as well as the master installation), as well as endpoint scanning. 
As a part of the process, vendor documentation is required to be retained and utilized when configuring productions systems.  Common vendor documentation includes administration guides, user guides, installation quick starts, and manuals. 

Note that multiple vendor documents may be provided, pending different services within an single enterprise level vendor solution.   Where multiple solutions are in use, multiple vendor documents are required. 
If websites / URLs are appropriate, provide the entire URL path (versus a general website, and the QSA has to dig for the correct documentation).  Where a URL is provided, ensure that an organizational login is not required to retrieve the indicated vendor documentation. 

Documentation must address both the master installation of the solution (i.e.. management console), as well as the software deployed to the in scope endpoints. Documentation on anti-malware / anti-malware process must include content which addresses:
-how the solution is configured to perform automatic updates 
-how the solution is configured to perform periodic scans. 

Every possible measure should be taken to ensure that system compromise is prevented at the lowest possible level.  Ensuring that all systems commonly affected by viruses and malware are protected includes ensuring that the protection measures are updated to address emerging threats.  Ensure that the documentation requiring not only installation, but also updating of all anti-virus, or anti-malware technologies is kept current and reflects the organizations security plans.
(ROC|5.2.2)

requests.raw_requirement: Policies and procedures regarding anti-malware
requests.request_text:
Provide formal internal anti-malware process documentation that discusses how anti-malware software and definitions are implemented and kept up-to-date.
Documentation must address both the master installation of the solution (i.e. management console), as well as the software deployed to the in scope endpoints.   Documentation on anti-malware / anti-malware process must include content which addresses: 
-how the solution is configured to perform automatic updates 
-how the solution is configured to perform periodic scans. 

Every possible measure should be taken to ensure that system compromise is prevented at the lowest possible level.  Ensuring that all systems commonly affected by viruses and malware are protected includes ensuring that the protection measures are updated to address emerging threats.  Ensure that the documentation requiring not only installation, but also updating of all anti-virus, or anti-malware technologies is kept current and reflects the organizations security plans.
(ROC|5.3.1.b)

requests.raw_requirement: Policies and procedures for vulnerability identification and risk ranking
requests.request_text:
Note that this requirement can also apply to internally developed software applications, but would not correctly address the requirements for software development. 
Documentation must include content which addresses:
-how the organization stays informed of potential threats and vulnerabilities for in-scope systems and applications 
-how the organization assigns a risk ranking to vulnerabilities, including how to identify / categorize  all high risk and critical vulnerabilities. 
-how the organization uses reputable outside sources for security vulnerability information 
- what are those resources and how is the information procured (email distro, notifications within a management console, etc.). 

And includes at least the following: 
-the installation of applicable critical vendor-supplied security patches within one month of release. 
-the installation of all applicable vendor-supplied security patches within an appropriate time frame (for example, within three months). 

Note that the patch and update practices could logically coincide with the quarterly vulnerability scanning requirements, as the vulnerability scanning efforts often identify missing patches and security updates.
(ROC|6.3.1.a)

requests.raw_requirement: Software development for internal and external software applications
requests.request_text:
Documentation on software development practices must include content which addresses: 
-secure development in accordance with PCI DSS (for example, secure authentication and logging). 
-based on industry standards and/or best practices. 
-the inclusion of information security throughout the software development life cycle. 

And includes at least the following: 
-pre-production and/or custom application accounts, user IDs and/or passwords are removed before an application goes into production or is released to customers 
-code changes are reviewed by individuals other than the originating code author (using either manual or automated processes), and 
-code changes are reviewed by individuals knowledgeable about code review techniques and secure coding practices. 
-code reviews ensure code is developed according to secure coding guidelines (see PCI DSS Requirement 6.5). 
-appropriate corrections are implemented prior to code release. 
-code review results are reviewed and approved by management prior to code release. 

Similar to including information security steps within policies, procedures, or standards, all documentation surrounding software development must clearly discuss how software must adhere to, and be developed in a manner that allows it to appropriately comply with the PCI DSS standard. 

While developing and testing applications, it will be necessary to have accounts to run quality assurance tests, and function tests.  It is key to security of such systems that all test accounts are removed prior to release into a production, and/or customer environment. 

Through either a manual or automated manner, all changes to custom code must be reviewed to ensure that security is being implemented accordingly. 

Within the security community, multiple independent bodies have developed best practices for developing software.  One example would be OWASP.  Within your development policies, procedures, or standards insure that the framework that is being followed/emulated is listed, and that adequate information is provided for readers to understand the goals of the framework. 

For Significant Changes 
Having processes to analyze significant changes helps ensure that all appropriate PCI DSS controls are applied to any systems or networks added or changed within the in-scope environment. 
Building this validation into the change-management processes helps ensure that device inventories and configuration standards are kept up-to-date and security controls are applied where needed. 
A change management process should include supporting evidence that PCI DSS requirements are implemented or preserved through the iterative process. Examples of PCI DSS requirements that could be impacted include, but are not limited to: 
-Network diagram is updated to reflect changes. 
-Systems are configured per configuration standards, with all default passwords changed and unnecessary services disabled. 
-Systems are protected with required controls e.g., file-integrity monitoring (FIM), anti-virus, patches, audit logging. 
-Sensitive authentication data (SAD) is not stored and all cardholder data (CHD) storage is documented and incorporated into data-retention policy and procedures 
-New systems are included in the quarterly vulnerability scanning process.
(ROC|6.2.1, 6.2.2.a, 6.2.3.a, 6.2.3.1.a, 8.6.2.a)

requests.raw_requirement: Change records for significant changes include PCI DSS control updates
requests.request_text:
Having processes to analyze significant changes helps ensure that all appropriate PCI DSS controls are applied to any systems or networks added or changed within the in-scope environment. 
Building this validation into the change-management processes helps ensure that device inventories and configuration standards are kept up-to-date and security controls are applied where needed. 
A change management process should include supporting evidence that PCI DSS requirements are implemented or preserved through the iterative process. Examples of PCI DSS requirements that could be impacted include, but are not limited to: 
 - Network diagram is updated to reflect changes. 
 - Systems are configured per configuration standards, with all default passwords changed and unnecessary services disabled. 
 - Systems are protected with required controls., file-integrity monitoring (FIM), anti-virus, patches, audit logging. 
 - Sensitive authentication data (SAD) is not stored and all cardholder data (CHD) storage is documented and incorporated into data-retention policy and procedures 
 - New systems are included in the quarterly vulnerability scanning process.
(ROC|6.5.2, 11.3.1.3.a, 11.3.2.1.a)

requests.raw_requirement: Software development practices
requests.request_text:
Documentation on software development practices must include content which addresses: 
-that developers are trained at least annually in up-to-date secure coding techniques, including how to avoid common coding vulnerabilities. 
-that application development is based on secure coding guidelines. 

And includes at least the following: 
-on what industry best practices and guidance the developer training is based. 

All personnel developing, or working within a development capacity, must undergo training related to secure-coding techniques. 

Secure Coding 
The application layer is high-risk and may be targeted by both internal and external threats. 
Requirements 6.5.1 through 6.5.10 are the minimum controls that should be in place, and organizations should incorporate the relevant secure-coding practices as applicable to the particular technology in their environment.
Application developers should be properly trained to identify and resolve issues related to these (and other) common coding vulnerabilities. Having staff knowledgeable of secure-coding guidelines should minimize the number of security vulnerabilities introduced through poor coding practices. Training for developers may be provided in-house or by third parties and should be applicable for technology used.
As industry-accepted secure-coding practices change, organizational coding practices and developer training should likewise be updated to address new threats for example, memory scraping attacks.
The vulnerabilities identified in 6.5.1 through 6.5.10 provide a minimum baseline. It is up to the organization to remain up to date with vulnerability trends and incorporate appropriate measures into their secure-coding practices.

Ensure that, at minimum, the following items are maintained:
-Training material presented 
-Credentials of the personnel providing the training, 
-Adequate attendance roster to confirm that all developers were present, and 
-An acknowledgment of developers stating that they understood the material.
(ROC|6.2.4)

requests.raw_requirement: Software developers receive training annually on secure-coding techniques
requests.request_text:
The application layer is high-risk and may be targeted by both internal and external threats.
Requirements 6.5.1 through 6.5.10 are the minimum controls that should be in place, and organizations should incorporate the relevant secure-coding practices as applicable to the particular technology in their environment. 
Application developers should be properly trained to identify and resolve issues related to these (and other) common coding vulnerabilities. Having staff knowledgeable of secure-coding guidelines should minimize the number of security vulnerabilities introduced through poor coding practices. Training for developers may be provided in-house or by third parties and should be applicable for technology used. 
As industry-accepted secure-coding practices change, organizational coding practices and developer training should likewise be updated to address new threats for example, memory scraping attacks. 
The vulnerabilities identified in 6.5.1 through 6.5.10 provide a minimum baseline. It is up to the organization to remain up to date with vulnerability trends and incorporate appropriate measures into their secure-coding practices.
(ROC|6.2.2.b)

requests.raw_requirement: Securing public-facing web applications
requests.request_text:
New threats and vulnerabilities must be addressed on an ongoing basis and these applications must be protected against known attacks by either of the following methods: 
-Implementation of manual or automated application vulnerability security assessment tools or methods, at least annually and after any changes.  
-Installation of an automated technical solution that detects and prevents web-based attacks (for example, a web-application firewall) in front of public-facing web applications, to continually check all traffic. 

For application vulnerability security assessments, documentation must include content which addresses: 
-that public-facing web applications are reviewed using the tools and/or methods indicated above, as follows: 
-At least annually. 
-After any changes. 
-By an organization that specializes in application security. 
-That, at a minimum, all vulnerabilities in Requirement 6.5 are included in the assessment. 
-That all vulnerabilities are corrected 
-That the application is re-evaluated after the corrections 

For automated technical solutions, documentation must include content which addresses: 
-that the automated technical solution is in place as follows: 
-situated in front of public-facing web applications to detect and prevent web-based attacks. 
-actively running and up-to-date as applicable. 
-generating audit logs. 
-configured to either block web-based attacks, or generate an alert that is immediately investigated.
(ROC|6.4.1)

requests.raw_requirement: Policies and procedures for access provisioning with job roles
requests.request_text:
Documentation must include content which addresses: 
 - identifying business roles requiring access to in-scope system components and CHD 
 - Defining access needs and privilege assignments for each role. 
 - Restriction of access to privileged user IDs to least privileges necessary to perform job responsibilities. 
 - Assignment of access based on individual personnels job classification and function 
 - Documented approval (electronically or in writing) by authorized parties for all access, including listing of specific privileges approved. 
 - The more people who have access to cardholder data, the more risk there is that a users account will be used maliciously. Limiting access to those with a legitimate business reason for the access helps an organization prevent mishandling of cardholder data through inexperience or malice.
(ROC|7.1.1, 7.2.1.a, 7.2.1.b, 7.2.2.a, 7.2.3.a, 7.2.4.a, 7.2.5.a, 7.2.5.1.a, 7.2.6.a)

requests.raw_requirement: Customer (non-consumer) guidance for password management
requests.request_text:
Service providers only - without account-lockout mechanisms in place, an attacker can continuously attempt to guess a password through manual or automated tools (e.g., password cracking), until they achieve success and gain access to a users account.  Ensure that non-consumer accounts are locked out after no more than six invalid attempts. 

Non-consumer users are different than third-party vendors.  This requirement is not referring to access to the CDE itself. 
Non-consumer customer users are any user accounts on in-scope systems or applications (e.g., web portals), used by the service providers customers who are not cardholders.
For instance, if the service provider being assessed is a payment gateway, they may have a customer portal where their clients (merchants) log in to review sales and transaction histories. Those users are the service providers customers, but they are not the cardholders (consumers) who made the purchases. This makes them non-consumer customers of the service provider.

Ensure that non-consumer passwords are required to meet at least the following strength/complexity:
-minimum length of at least twelve characters. 
-non-consumer user passwords are required to contain both numeric and alphabetic characters. 

Ensure that non-consumer passwords are required to be changed and at a minimum the following are documented: 
-non-consumer customer user passwords are required to change periodically; and 
-non-consumer customer users are given guidance as to when, and under what circumstances, passwords must change.

Ensure that adequate documentation exists that requires that non-consumer passwords are tracked for history to ensure that passwords are different from the previous four passwords used.
(ROC|8.3.10)

requests.raw_requirement: Policies and procedures for Identity and access management
requests.request_text:
Documentation must include content which addresses: 
-the assignment of a unique ID to all users before allowing them to access system components or cardholder data. 
-the process to control addition, deletion, and modification of user IDs, credentials, and other identifier objects. 
-that access is immediately revoked for any terminated users. 
-that inactive user accounts are removed / disabled at least every 90 days. 

The management of IDs used by vendors to access, support, or maintain system components via remote access as follows: 
-Enabled only during the time period needed and disabled when not in use.  
-Monitored when in use.  
-Limit repeated access attempts by locking out the user ID after not more than six attempts. 
-Set the lockout duration to a minimum of 30 minutes or until an administrator enables the user ID. 
-If a session has been idle for more than 15 minutes, require the user to re-authenticate to re-activate the terminal or session. 
-users must be authenticated using a unique ID and additional authentication for access to the cardholder data environment. 
-how the organization handles requests by users for modifying (changing, unlocking) authentication credentials, including that if a user requests a reset of an authentication credential by a non-face-to-face method, the users identity is verified before the authentication credential is modified.

How the organization handles password management of their users, including that: 
-First-time passwords/passphrases must be set to a unique value for each user. 
-First-time passwords/passphrases must be changed after the first use. 
-Reset passwords/passphrases must be set to a unique value for each user. 
-Reset passwords/passphrases must be changed after the first use. 

How the organization communicates (distribution of a policy, etc.) and advises personnel to manage their individual credentials, including: 
-Guidance on selecting strong authentication credentials. 
-Guidance for how users should protect their authentication credentials. 
-Instructions for users not to reuse previously used passwords. 
-That users should change passwords if there is any suspicion the password could be compromised. 
-that use of group and shared IDs and/or passwords or other authentication methods are explicitly prohibited. 

If other authentication mechanisms are used (for example, physical or logical security tokens, smart cards, certificates, etc.) documentation must include: 
-Authentication mechanisms are assigned to an individual account and not shared among multiple accounts. 
-Physical and/or logical controls are defined to ensure only the intended account can use that mechanism to gain access. 
-In order to ensure that the organization can track all access to Cardholder Data (CHD) back to specific individuals, each user with access must have a unique account.
(ROC|8.3.1, 8.3.4, 8.3.5)

requests.raw_requirement: Policies and procedures for management of non-consumer customer passwords.
requests.request_text:
Service Providers only - Ensure that non-consumer passwords are required to meet at least the following strength/complexity: 
 - A minimum length of at least seven characters. 
 - non-consumer user passwords are required to contain both numeric and alphabetic characters.
 - non-consumer customer user passwords are required to change periodically; and 
 - non-consumer customer users are given guidance as to when, and under what circumstances, passwords must change.
 - Ensure that adequate documentation exists that requires that non-consumer passwords are tracked for history to ensure that passwords are different from the previous four passwords used.
(ROC|8.3.6, 8.3.8, 8.3.9, 8.3.10, 8.6.3)

requests.raw_requirement: Password procedures related to first-time and existing user password resets
requests.request_text:
Ensure that passwords meet the following requirements when initially set: 
 - First-time passwords must be set to a unique value for each user. 
 - First-time passwords must be changed after the first use. 
 - Reset passwords must be set to a unique value for each user. 
 - Reset passwords must be changed after the first use.
(ROC|8.3.5)

requests.raw_requirement: Physical locations are secured and protected from unauthorized access
requests.request_text:
Documentation must include content which addresses how the organization distinguishes between visitors and authorized personnel, including: 
 - identifying onsite personnel and visitors (e.g., assigning badges) 
 - changing access requirements
 - revoking terminated onsite personnel and expired visitor identification (e.g., ID badges). 
 - in areas where cardholder data is processed or maintained, that visitors must be authorized before they are granted access to, and escorted at all times while in, these areas. 
 - where a security system is in use, how the organization ensures that only authorized personnel are able to administer those systems.
(ROC|9.2.1.a)

requests.raw_requirement: Management and physical security of media with stored CHD
requests.request_text:
Documentation must include content which addresses: 
-how the organization physically secures media with CHD (including but not limited to computers, removable electronic media, paper receipts, paper reports, and faxes). 
-how the organization verifies the security of the storage location, required to be done annually (note that if a third party hosting location is used, an AOC may be sufficient here.  Consult your QSA to confirm.) 
-how the distribution of media is controlled, including media that is distributed to individuals. 
-how media is classified so that the sensitivity of the data can be determined 
-how media is inventoried, and review / updated periodically 
-that hard-copy materials must be crosscut shredded, incinerated, or pulped such that there is reasonable assurance the hard-copy materials cannot be reconstructed. 
-that storage containers used for materials that are to be destroyed must be secured. 
-that CHD on electronic media must be rendered unrecoverable (e.g. via a secure wipe program in accordance with industry-accepted standards for secure deletion, or by physically destroying the media). 
-if data is rendered unrecoverable via secure deletion or a secure wipe program, identify the industry-accepted standards used. 

Tracking the media with CHD throughout its life cycle, including authorized personnel access, and upon dissemination to other parties is critical to compliance. 

Information about your media classification scheme is important so that media containing CHD is considered "confidential" and afforded appropriate protection. 
The classification status of media should be easily discernible to personnel (but does not necessarily need to be physically labeled). 

Ensure that devices within the environment that are associated with capturing Cardholder Data (CHD) are inventoried, and that the requirements for, and procedures for how to carry out inventories are within policies, procedures, and standards. 
Without careful inventory methods and storage controls, stolen or missing media could go unnoticed for an indefinite amount of time. 
If media is not inventoried, stolen or lost media may not be noticed for a long time or at all.  
If steps are not taken to destroy information contained on hard disks, portable drives, CD/DVDs, or paper prior to disposal, malicious individuals may be able to retrieve information from the disposed media, leading to a data compromise. 

Securing storage containers used for materials that are going to be destroyed prevents sensitive information from being captured while the materials are being collected. 

Examples of methods for securely destroying electronic media include secure wiping, degaussing, or physical destruction (such as grinding or shredding hard disks).
(ROC|9.4.1, 9.4.2.b,  9.4.1.1.b, 9.4.1.2, 9.4.3.b, 9.4.3.c, 9.4.4.b,  9.4.5.b, 9.4.5.1, 9.4.7)

requests.raw_requirement: Population of media containing account data
requests.request_text:
Inventory of all media that contains account data. This may include physical or electronic media such as USB storage, backup tapes, or hard drives. The inventory should include identifying information, media type, location at a minimum.
(ROC|9.4.3, 9.4.5)

requests.raw_requirement: Management and protection of payment capture devices
requests.request_text:
Documentation must include content which addresses: 
 - that an inventory of devices is maintained. 
 - devices are periodically inspected to look for tampering or substitution. 
 - personnel are trained to be aware of suspicious behavior and to report tampering or substitution of POS devices. 
 - procedures for inspecting devices 
 - frequency of inspections
 - Keeping an up-to-date list of devices helps an organization keep track of where devices are supposed to be, and quickly identify if a device is missing or lost. 
 - The method for maintaining a list of devices may be automated (e.g., a device-management system) or manual (e.g., documented in electronic or paper records). For on-the-road devices, the location may include the name of the personnel to whom the device is assigned.
(ROC|9.5.1)

requests.raw_requirement: Managing time synchronization technologies
requests.request_text:
Documentation must include content which addresses: 
 - that time synchronization technology is implemented and kept current per PCI DSS Requirements 6.1 and 6.2 (i.e. security patch and update requirements ensuring security vulnerabilities are known and addressed with the required time periods. See Requirement 6.1 and 6.2 for more details). 
 - Time-synchronization technology is used to synchronize clocks on multiple systems. When clocks are not properly synchronized, it can be difficult, if not impossible, to compare log files from different systems and establish an exact sequence of events (crucial for forensic Additional Evidence Request in the event of a breach). For post-incident forensics teams, the accuracy and consistency of time across all systems and the time of each activity is critical in determining how the systems were compromised.
(ROC|10.1.1)

requests.raw_requirement: Capture, review, and retention of logs for in-scope systems and applications
requests.request_text:
Documentation must include content which addresses: 
-daily log reviews on security and audit logs of all in-scope systems and applications. 
-periodic reviews of logs from all other systems, based on the organizations policies and risk management strategy 
-how the organization follows up on exceptions and anomalies identified during the review process.   
-the organization's audit log retention policies. 
-that audit logs are retained for at least one year, with a minimum of three months immediately available online. 

Reviews should include:
-All security events; 
-Logs of all system components that store, process, or transmit CHD and/or SAD; 
-Logs of all critical system components; and 
-Logs of all servers and system components that perform security functions. 

Note that log reviews can be completed either manually or via log tools / correlation engines.
(ROC|10.4.1.1, 10.4.2.b, 10.4.2.1.b)

requests.raw_requirement: Quarterly detection and identification of authorized and unauthorized wireless access points
requests.request_text:
Documentation must include content which addresses: 
-how unauthorized wireless access points are detected 
-how both unauthorized and authorized wireless access points are identified / confirmed 
-that this process occurs on a quarterly basis.  

Note that methods that may be used in the process include but are not limited to wireless network scans, physical/logical inspections of system components and infrastructure, network access control (NAC), or wireless IDS/IPS. 
Whichever methods are used, they must be sufficient to detect and identify both authorized and unauthorized devices.
(ROC|11.2.1.a, 11.2.1.d)

requests.raw_requirement: Policies and procedures for incident response
requests.request_text:
Documentation must include content which addresses: 
-that the incident response plan must be immediately implemented in the event of system breach. 
-the plan is tested at least annually. 
-specific personnel are designated to be available on a 24/7 basis to respond to alerts: 
-24/7 incident monitoring 
-24/7 incident response 
-appropriate training is provided to staff with security breach response responsibilities. 
-alerts from security monitoring systems are monitored, including but not limited to intrusion-detection, intrusion-prevention, firewalls, and file-integrity monitoring systems, and action taken (as needed) 
-the process to modify and evolve the incident response plan according to lessons learned and to incorporate industry developments.
