The incident response plan should be thorough and contain all the key elements to allow your company to respond effectively in the event of a breach that could impact cardholder data. 

The QSA should clearly see: 
-Roles and responsibilities. 
-Communication strategies. 
-Requirement for notification of the payment brands. 
-Specific incident response procedures. 
-Business recovery and continuity procedures. 
-Data back-up processes. 
-Analysis of legal requirements for reporting compromises. 
-Coverage for all critical system components. 
-Responses for all critical system components. 
-Reference or inclusion of incident response procedures from the payment brands. 
-Designation that personnel are available on a 24/7 basis to respond to security incidents.
-Testing and training requirements

Also, specific to rogue wireless access points:
-how the organization responds to detection of an unauthorized wireless access point.
(ROC|10.7.1.a, 10.7.2.a, 10.7.3.a, 11.5.1.1.a, 11.5.1.1.b, 12.10.1.a, 12.10.2, 12.10.3, 12.10.4, 12.10.4.1, 12.10.5, 12.10.6.a, 12.10.6.b, 12.10.7.a)

requests.raw_requirement: Policies and procedures for network vulnerability scanning
requests.request_text:
Documentation on network vulnerability scanning processes must include content which addresses: 
 - performing internal and external network vulnerability scans at least quarterly and after any significant change in the network (such as new system component installations, changes in network topology, firewall rule modifications, product upgrades). 
 - re-scans are performed to verify all high-risk vulnerabilities are resolved in accordance with the entity's vulnerability ranking (per Requirement 6.1)
(ROC|11.1.1)

requests.raw_requirement: Supplemental vulnerability scans for internal and external environments
requests.request_text:
Ensure that policies, procedures, and standards require appropriate internal and external scans when significant changes are made within the environment such as new system component installations, changes in network topology, firewall rule modifications, and product upgrades.
(ROC|11.1.1)

requests.raw_requirement: Methodology for penetration testing
requests.request_text:
Documentation on penetration testing processes must include content which addresses:
 - how the methodology is based on industry-accepted penetration testing approaches. 
 - coverage for the entire CDE, perimeter and critical systems. 
 - testing is performed from both inside ("internal") and outside ("external") the network. 
 - testing is performed to validate any segmentation and scope reduction controls. 
 - application-layer penetration testing is performed and includes, at a minimum, the vulnerabilities listed in Requirement 6.5. 
 - network-layer penetration tests are performed to include components that support network functions as well as operating systems. 
 - there is review and consideration of threats and vulnerabilities experienced in the last 12 months. 
 - how the results of penetration testing as well as any associated remediation activities are retained 
 - that Penetration Testing (internal, external, application-layer and segmentation) must be performed per the defined methodology no less than annually. 
 - that exploitable vulnerabilities identified from Penetration Testing must be corrected, and repeated testing must be performed to confirm vulnerabilities are corrected. 
 - that Penetration Testing must be re-performed after any significant change such as an operating system upgrade, a sub-network added to the environment, or a web server added to the environment (as applicable, and per the scope of the change).
(ROC|11.4.1, 11.4.2, 11.4.3, 11.4.4)

requests.raw_requirement: Penetration testing results (internal, external, application-layer and segmentation)
requests.request_text:
Merchants are required to maintain documented results from the most recent penetration test, which includes: 
-Penetration testing is performed to verify segmentation controls at least annually and after any changes to segmentation controls/methods. 
-The penetration testing covers all segmentation controls/methods in use. 
-The penetration testing verifies that segmentation controls/methods are operational and effective, and isolate all out-of-scope systems from systems in the CDE.
(ROC|11.4.2.a, 11.4.3.a, 11.4.4)

requests.raw_requirement: Post significant change penetration testing results
requests.request_text:
Penetration testing reporting document that detail the results of testing conducted after a significant change. This testing can be the same as annual testing but must be performed after significant change and include all changed components.
(ROC|11.4.4)

requests.raw_requirement: Policies and procedures for information security for the organization
requests.request_text:
In order to ensure that the organization is properly maintaining an information security program that adequately addresses the concerns of PCI DSS, an overall information security policy must be established and maintained.

Further, the policy must also:
-be reviewed at least annually 
-updated as needed to reflect changes to business objectives or the risk environment. 
-clearly define information security responsibilities for all personnel 
-formally assign information security to a Chief Security Officer or other security-knowledgeable member of management. 

Formally assign the following information security responsibilities: 
-Establish, document, and distribute security policies and procedures. 
-Monitor and analyze security alerts and information, and distribute to appropriate personnel. 
-Establish, document, and distribute security incident response and escalation procedures to ensure timely and effective handling of all situations. 
-Administer user accounts, including additions, deletions, and modifications. 
-Monitor and control all access to data. 

Note that the required documentation for information security could also (but does not have to) encompass content for acceptable use, and security awareness training. 

If there are different information security, etc. documents for different audiences (i.e. technical staff, operational / retail / restaurant staff, third party vendors, etc.)
(ROC|12.1.1, 12.1.2, 12.1.3.a, 12.1.4)

requests.raw_requirement: Policies and procedures for the security awareness and education program.
requests.request_text:
Documentation must include content which addresses: 
 - that multiple methods of communicating awareness / educating personnel are used by the organization (and what those are) 
 - Personnel attend security awareness training: 
 - Upon hire, and 
 - At least annually 
 - Personnel acknowledge, in writing or electronically and at least annually, that they have read and understand the information security policy.
(ROC|12.6.1)

requests.raw_requirement: Vendor management for managing contracted third-parties
requests.request_text:
Documentation must include content which addresses: 
 - maintenance of a list of service providers. 
 - maintenance of a written agreement for each service provider that includes an acknowledgement that the service providers will maintain all applicable PCI DSS requirements to the extent the service provider handles, has access to, or otherwise stores, processes, or transmits the customers cardholder data or sensitive authentication data, or manages the customer's cardholder data environment on behalf of a customer. 
 - ensure that there is an established process for engaging service providers including proper due diligence prior to engagement. 
 - maintenance of a program to monitor service providers PCI DSS compliance status at least annually. 
 - maintenance of information about which PCI DSS requirements are managed by each service provider, and which are managed by the entity. 
 
According to the PCI DSS, a service provider is: 
 - Business entity that is not a payment brand, directly involved in the processing, storage, or transmission of cardholder data on behalf of another entity. This also includes companies that provide services that control or could impact the security of cardholder data. Examples include managed service providers that provide managed firewalls, IDS, and other services as well as hosting providers and other entities. If an entity provides a service that involves only the provision of public network access such as a telecommunications company providing just the communication link the entity would not be considered a service provider for that service (although they may be considered a service provider for other services).
(ROC|12.8.1.a, 12.8.2.a, 12.8.3.a, 12.8.4.a, 12.8.5.a)

requests.raw_requirement: Incident response plan annual test
requests.request_text:
Verify that the organization is prepared to respond immediately to a system breach, with defined processes as follows: 
-Create the incident response plan to be implemented in the event of a system breach. 
-Test the plan to be implemented in the event of a system breach at least annually.  

Ensure that the test addresses the following, at a minimum: 
-Roles, responsibilities, and communication and contact strategies in the event of a compromise including notification of the payment brands, at a minimum.  
-Specific incident response procedures.  
-Business recovery and continuity procedures. Data back-up processes.

-Analysis of legal requirements for reporting compromises.  
-Coverage and responses of all critical system components.  
-Reference or inclusion of incident response procedures from the payment brands.  
-Designate specific personnel to be available on a 24/7 basis to respond to alerts (24/7 incident monitoring, 24/7 incident response).  
-Provide appropriate training to staff with security breach response responsibilities.  
-Include alerts from security monitoring systems, including but not limited to intrusion-detection, intrusion-prevention, firewalls, and file integrity monitoring systems.  
-Develop a process to modify and evolve the incident response plan according to lessons learned and to incorporate industry developments.
(ROC|12.10.2)

requests.raw_requirement: Targeted risk assessment process
requests.request_text:
Each PCI DSS requirement that provides flexibility for how frequently it is performed (for example, requirements to be performed periodically) is supported by a targeted risk analysis that is documented and includes: 
- Identification of the assets being protected. 
- Identification of the threat(s) that the requirement is protecting against.
- Identification of factors that contribute to the likelihood and/or impact of a threat being realized. 
- Resulting analysis that determines, and includes justification for, how frequently the requirement must be performed to minimize the likelihood of the threat being realized.
- Review of each targeted risk analysis at least once every 12 months to determine whether the results are still valid or if an updated risk analysis is needed.
- Performance of updated risk analyses when needed, as determined by the annual review.
(ROC|12.3.1)

requests.raw_requirement: Acceptable use policies for end-user technologies
requests.request_text:
Note: Examples of critical technologies include, but are not limited to, remote access and wireless technologies, laptops (and other systems and devices), tablets, removable electronic media, e-mail usage and Internet usage. 

Documentation must include content which addresses: 
-Explicit approval from authorized parties to use the technologies. 
-All technology use is to be authenticated with user ID and password or other authentication item. 
-A list of all devices and personnel authorized to use the devices must be maintained. 
-A method to accurately and readily determine owner, contact information, and purpose must be implemented. 
-what are the acceptable uses for the technology. 
-what are the acceptable network locations for the technology. 
-that the organization manages a list of company-approved products. 
-that there is automatic disconnect of sessions for remote-access technologies after a specific period of inactivity, and what this / these times are (for each). 
-there is activation of remote-access technologies used by vendors and business partners only when needed by vendors and business partners, with immediate deactivation after use. 
-that copying, moving, or storing of cardholder data onto local hard drives and removable electronic media is prohibited when accessing such data via remote-access technologies.. 

Note that the required documentation for information security could also (but does not have to) encompass content for acceptable use, and security awareness training. 
If there are different information security, etc. documents for different audiences (i.e.. technical staff, operational / retail / restaurant staff, third party vendors, etc.) please provide that documentation.
(ROC|12.2.1)

requests.raw_requirement: Last four quarters of internal vulnerability scans and rescans
requests.request_text:
A robust scanning program ensures that scans are performed and vulnerabilities addressed in a timely manner. 
- Quarterly scans should be performed every 90 days on all in-scope components and networks.  Rescans must occur if any vulnerabilities are identified and remediation actions are taken.  If vulnerabilities are not remediated, and rescans are not completed, a business justification must be documented and a compensating control worksheet may be required. 
- Note that any change control documentation - change tickets, etc. - associated with vulnerability scan remediation validation should be presented to your QSA for evidence review. 
- Note that (4) quarterly internal scans are expected to have been performed in the past 12-month period.
(ROC|11.3.1.a, 11.3.1.b, 11.3.1.1.a, 11.3.1.2.b, 11.3.1.3.a, 11.3.1.3.b)

requests.raw_requirement: Intrusion-detection and intrusion-prevention techniques and solutions are configured, maintained, and updated
requests.request_text:
PCI requires that organizations implement IDS / IPS techniques and solutions in accordance with vendor documentation to ensure they are optimized and operating correctly, including configuration, maintenance and updates. 
As a part of the process, vendor documentation is required to be retained and utilized when configuring production systems.  Common vendor documentation includes administration guides, user guides, installation quick starts, and manuals. 

Note that multiple vendor documents may be provided, pending different services within an single enterprise level vendor solution.   Where multiple solutions are in use, multiple vendor documents are required.
If websites / URLs are appropriate, provide the entire URL path (versus a general website, and the QSA has to dig for the correct documentation).  Where a URL is provided, ensure that an organizational login is not required to retrieve the indicated vendor documentation.  

Note that an IDS / IPS appliance / solution itself is not required, as long as there is an IDS IPS technique implemented in accordance with the intent of the DSS, as noted below: 
- Intrusion detection and/or intrusion prevention techniques (such as IDS/IPS) compare the traffic coming into the network with known signatures and/or behaviors of thousands of compromise types (hacker tools, Trojans, and other malware), and send alerts and/or stop the attempt as it happens. 
- Without a proactive approach to unauthorized activity detection, attacks on (or misuse of) computer resources could go unnoticed in real time. Security alerts generated by these techniques should be monitored so that the attempted intrusions can be stopped.
(ROC|11.5.1.c)

requests.raw_requirement: Monitored files and logs from in-scope components showing change detection and FIM is active
requests.request_text:
Examples of files that should be monitored include (but are not limited to): 
-System executables 
-Application executables 
-Configuration and parameter files 
-Centrally stored, historical or archived, log and audit files 
-Additional critical files determined by entity (i.e., through risk assessment or other means) 

As part of a PCI engagement, the Coalfire QSA is required to review information about the organization's processes around being alerted and responding to file integrity (FIM) / change detection events on relevant files of in-scope systems and devices, which might indicate unauthorized changes have occurred. 
Evidence is required to show that the organization has defined processes for timely detection of, and  response to, events related to unauthorized changes.
(ROC|10.3.4, 11.5.2.a, 11.6.1.a, 11.6.1.c)

requests.raw_requirement: Background checks for access to CDE systems and data
requests.request_text:
Redacted results of background checks for personnel managing technology for the card environment, as well as, personnel handling / access more than one credit/debit card at a time.
(ROC|12.7.1)

requests.raw_requirement: List of all previously reported incidents
requests.request_text:
List of cybersecurity incidents that the organization has recorded and responded to in the past 12 months. The QSA will select a sample from this listing and request additional details.
(ROC|12.10.1.b, 12.10.7.b)

requests.raw_requirement: List of all terminated personnel within the last six months that had access to the CDE
requests.request_text:
As part of a PCI engagement, the Coalfire QSA is required to obtain and review a sample of terminated personnel in the past six months to confirm if they were handled in accordance with its termination policies and procedures. 

Providing the full listing of all terminated users; the QSA will then select a subset for inclusion within the assessment report.
(ROC|8.2.5, 9.3.1.1.c)

requests.raw_requirement: Logged audit events for each in-scope system, device and data repository
requests.request_text:
Discuss with your QSA the best way to provide this evidence, as there could be different methods.  The following types of events must be logged for all in-scope systems, devices and applications: 
-All individual access to CHD (Note that this could be file shares, databases, mainframes, etc.)  
-All actions taken by any individual with root or administrative privileges. 
-Access to all audit trails. 
-Invalid logical access attempts. 
-Use of and changes to identification and authentication mechanisms, including: All elevation of privileges. All changes, additions, or deletions to any account with root or administrative privileges. 
-Initialization of audit logs.  
-Stopping or pausing of audit logs. 
-Creation and deletion of system level objects.  
-Any changes to time settings on critical systems are logged. 

And for each event log entry, the following metadata must be captured (at a minimum): 
-User identification 
-Type of event 
-Date and time 
-Success or failure indication 
-Origination of event
(ROC|10.2.1.1, 10.2.1.2, 10.2.1.3, 10.2.1.4, 10.2.1.5, 10.2.1.6, 10.2.1.7, 10.2.2, 10.3.4, 10.3.4)

requests.raw_requirement: Alerted for new vulnerabilities for all in-scope systems, devices, and applications
requests.request_text:
As part of a PCI engagement, the Coalfire QSA is required to confirm that the organization's staff is being alerted to new security vulnerabilities for all of the technology components within its cardholder environment.
This commonly may include RSS feeds, emails, mailing lists, subscription services, notifications through management consoles, etc.  For this, you can upload screenshots, PDF files of emails, etc.
(ROC|6.3.1.b)

requests.raw_requirement: Lists of user IDs of all personnel with access to the development, test, and production environments
requests.request_text:
Listing of all personnel with access to the production and pre-production environments. The assessor will use it to confirm that there is a segregation of duties/roles and responsibilities between individuals that maintain access development/test environments and those that maintain access to production environments. 
(ROC|6.5.3.a)

requests.raw_requirement: Records for completed security awareness training
requests.request_text:
As part of a PCI engagement, the Coalfire QSA is required to confirm that the organization conducts security awareness training for personnel with access to cardholder data and systems both upon hire and at least annually. 
Common evidence provided includes exports from a learning management system, physical sign-offs, etc.
(ROC|12.6.3.a)

requests.raw_requirement: Listing of all network connection changes and all firewall/router/security group/ACL configuration changes
requests.request_text:
As part of a PCI engagement, the Coalfire QSA is required to obtain and review change control tickets within the cardholder environment for: 
-Network connection changes 
-Firewall/router/security group/ACL configuration changes 
-Providing the full listings of all applicable change control tickets for network connection and network configuration changes in the cardholder environment 

All change control tickets are required to include documentation to address each of the below change control components.
-Documentation of Impact statement
-Approval by authorized parties
-Back-out procedures
-Results of functionality and security testing

The QSA will then select a subset for inclusion within the assessment report.
(ROC|1.2.2.b)

requests.raw_requirement: Listing of all non-network changes (patching, updates, upgrades) for in-scope systems and applications
requests.request_text:
As part of a PCI engagement, the Coalfire QSA is required to obtain and review a sample of non-network change control tickets within the cardholder environment. 

Providing the full listing of all applicable change control tickets for non-network changes in the cardholder environment. 
All change control tickets are required to include documentation to address each of the below change control components.
-Documentation of Impact statement
-Approval by authorized parties
-Back-out procedures
-Results of functionality and security testing

The QSA will then select a subset for inclusion within the assessment report.
(ROC|6.2.3.b, 6.2.3.1.b, 6.5.1.b)

requests.raw_requirement: Vendor documentation and Internet sources that identify default vendor-supplied credentials
requests.request_text:
Vendor documentation (files or links to vendor online documentation) for all in-scope systems in the CDE and systems connected to the CDE or that provide security services such as FIM, Anti-Malware, Logging, etc.
(ROC|2.2.2.b)

requests.raw_requirement: Vendor documentation for wireless networking technologies implementation of strong encryption for authentication and transmission
requests.request_text:
PCI requires that organizations implement wireless networking technologies in accordance with vendor documentation and / or industry standards, to ensure that all are optimized and operating correctly, including configuration, authentication, maintenance and updates, as well as the changing of security-related wireless vendor defaults. 

Specifically, wireless networks must be configured with strong encryption for: 
-Access / authentication over wireless networks 
-Transmission of data over wireless networks 

As a part of the process, vendor documentation is required to be retained and utilized when configuring productions systems.  Common vendor documentation includes administration guides, user guides, installation quick starts, and manuals. 
Note that multiple vendor documents may be provided, pending different services within an single enterprise level vendor solution.   Where multiple solutions are in use, multiple vendor documents are required. 
If websites / URLs are appropriate, provide the entire URL path (versus a general website, and the QSA has to dig for the correct documentation).  Where a URL is provided, ensure that an organizational login is not required to retrieve the indicated vendor documentation.
(ROC|2.3.1.c)

requests.raw_requirement: (Sample) Access requests for personnel (new or current) with privileged or general access to the CDE, systems, devices, and applications, and/or CHD
requests.request_text:
Documented approval (for example, in writing or electronically) that shows  personnel authorization for  access and privileges are known and authorized by management, and that their access is necessary for their job function.
(ROC|7.2.2, 7.2.3)

requests.raw_requirement: Access control lists for personnel who have physical access to the organization's sensitive areas
requests.request_text:
Access control lists for  sensitive areas that show only authorized personnel with a legitimate business need are granted access.   

Note that Sensitive areas refers to any data center, server room, or any area that houses systems that store, process, or transmit cardholder data. This excludes public-facing areas where only point-of-sale terminals are present, such as the cashier areas in a retail store.
(ROC|9.3.1)

requests.raw_requirement: Records of media that is sent outside the facility via secured courier
requests.request_text:
Media transfer records that identify which media, when, and to where and to whom  media was transferred.
(ROC|9.4.3)

requests.raw_requirement: Training materials for personnel in POI environments
requests.request_text:
Training materials must include content which addresses: 
 - Verifying the identity of any third-party persons claiming to be repair or maintenance personnel, prior to granting them access to modify or troubleshoot devices.  
 - Not to install, replace, or return devices without verification.  
 - Being aware of suspicious behavior around devices (for example, attempts by unknown persons to unplug or open devices).  
 - Reporting all suspicious behavior to appropriate personnel (for example, a manager or security officer).  
 - Reporting tampering or substitution of devices
(ROC|9.5.1.3.a)

requests.raw_requirement: Breach response training and attendee records
requests.request_text:
Records of training conducted annually to address changes to incident response procedures for individuals that have responsibilities associated with the incident response plan and must be trained  at regular intervals on how to respond to security incidents.
(ROC|12.10.4)

requests.raw_requirement: Employee acknowledgement of the information security policy at hire and annually
requests.request_text:
As part of a PCI engagement, the Coalfire QSA is required to confirm that the organization provides an information security policy to all personnel on an annual basis, and that personnel are required to acknowledge that they have read and understood the security policy and procedures.  
Evidence may include paper acknowledgement forms, or utilization of an LMS where a electronic file is made available to a user for review and signoff.
(ROC|12.1.3.c)

requests.raw_requirement: (Sample) Configuration settings for data repositories
requests.request_text:
(Applicable only if the organization maintains storage of cardholder data) As part of a PCI engagement, the Coalfire QSA is required to review information about the organization's data repositories.  Data repositories commonly consist of databases or systems that store flat files, containing encrypted cardholder information. Dependent on the data repository, the customer should provide the following information.
-Evidence to confirm removal of vendor default user accounts (PCI DSS 2)
-Database schema (PCI DSS 3)
-Database Tables, Field, and Column Names (PCI DSS 3)
-Sample of Incoming transaction logs (PCI DSS 3)
-Sample of all types of logs recorded (transaction, history, debugging, etc) (PCI DSS 3)
-Sample of history files (PCI DSS 3)
-Sample of trace files (PCI DSS 3)
-Folder structures/locations for flat files (PCI DSS 3)
-Evidence to confirm data does not exceed retention requirements (PCI DSS 3)
-Evidence to confirm mechanism for securely removing card data that exceeds retention requirements (PCI DSS 3)
-Evidence to confirm user access to data repository (PCI DSS 7 | PCI DSS 8)
(ROC|ES Section 6)

requests.raw_requirement: (Sample) Technical configuration information for POI devices
requests.request_text:
(Applicable only for Merchants). As part of a PCI engagement, the Coalfire QSA is required to review information about the organization's payment capture terminals (Point of Interaction - POIs) within the cardholder environment.  These payment POIs commonly directly process and/or transmit cardholder information. 

Dependent on the POI, the customer is required to provide the following information. 
-Evidence of encryption (application policy / SRED) (PCI DSS 2) 
-Evidence of full listing of POIs owned by the customer across all locations (PCI DSS 9)
(ROC|ES Section 6)

requests.raw_requirement: (Sample) Physical locations
requests.request_text:
As part of a PCI engagement, the Coalfire QSA is required to review information about the organization's physical locations (retail sites, datacenters, warehouses, call centers, etc) within the cardholder environment.  These location commonly are directly responsible for storing, processing, and transmitting cardholder information, as well as, are connected to/impact the security of the cardholder environment.  

Dependent on the location, the customer is required to provide the following information: 
 - Evidence of visitor logs and retention (PCI DSS 9)
 - Evidence of access control components (badges (granting, revocation), cameras, etc) (PCI DSS 9)
 -Evidence that clearly identifies visitors vs company (authorized) personnel (PCI DSS 9)
(ROC|9.3.4.a, 9.3.4.b, 9.3.4.c)

requests.raw_requirement: (Sample) Configuration settings for software/applications
requests.request_text:
As part of a PCI engagement, the Coalfire QSA is required to review information about the organization's applications within the cardholder environment.  These payment applications commonly directly or indirectly store , process, and/or transmit cardholder information.  Dependent on the application, the customer is required to provide the following information.
-Evidence to confirm removal of vendor default user accounts (PCI DSS 2)
-Evidence of only required ports/services/protocols, as well as, removal of unnecessary functionality system configs (PCI DSS 2)
-Evidence of patching (PCI DSS 6)
-Evidence of centralized (or not) access control for technology (PCI DSS 7)
-Evidence of unique user listing (local and remote) (PCI DSS 8)
-Evidence of strong password policy components (invalid lockout, lockout time limit, min length, numeric and alphabetic, rotation, history) (PCI DSS 8)
-Evidence of settings showing that credentials are strongly protected in transmission (PCI DSS 8)
-Evidence of settings showing that credentials are strongly protected in storage on devices (PCI DSS 8)
(ROC|ES Section 6)

requests.raw_requirement: Configuration and hardening standards for network devices
requests.request_text:
Configuration Standard documents (policy/process, or other formal documentation) must include the following: 
 - That all vendor defaults (including default passwords on operating systems, software providing security services, application and system accounts, POS terminals, Simple Network Management Protocol (SNMP) community strings, etc.) are changed before a system is installed on the network. 
 - List what vendor defaults are changed 
 - That unnecessary default accounts (including accounts used by operating systems, security software, applications, systems, POS terminals, SNMP, etc.) are removed or disabled before a system is installed on the network. 
 - List what vendor defaults are removed 
 - Identify industry-accepted hardening standard(s) that the system configuration standards is consistent with.  
 - List industry-accepted hardening standard(s) 
 - That system configuration standards are updated as new vulnerability issues are identified.  
 - Indicate that this is done (must be stated in policy/process) 
 - That system configuration standards are applied when new systems are configured, and verified as being in place before a system is installed on the network  
 - Indicate that this is done (must be stated in policy/process) 
 - That all vendor-supplied defaults are changed, and unnecessary default accounts are eliminated 
 - Indicate that this is done (must be stated in policy/process) 
 - That only one primary function per server is implemented (to prevent functions that require different security levels from co-existing on the same server) 
 - The demonstrated primary function should reconcile with the CDE inventory provided during the pre-assessment phase 
 - That only necessary services, protocols, daemons, etc., are enabled as required for the function of the system; 
 - List enabled necessary services, protocols, daemons, etc. 
 - Implementing additional security features for any required services, protocols or daemons that are considered to be insecure 
 - List additional security features for any insecure services, protocols or daemons, etc. that are required 
 - Configuring system security parameters to prevent misuse 
 - List implemented security parameters to prevent misuse of systems 
 - Removing all unnecessary functionality, such as scripts, drivers, features, subsystems, file systems, and unnecessary web servers 
 - List removed default functionality (scripts, drivers, features, subsystems, file systems, and unnecessary web services) 
 - That common security parameter settings are applied  
 - List applied common security parameter settings 
 - That enabled functions are documented  
 - The enabled functions should support what is indicated in the CDE inventory provided during the pre-assessment phase
(ROC|1.2.1.a, 1.3.1.a, 1.3.2.a, 1.4.1.a, 1.5.1.a)

requests.raw_requirement: Incident response plan and procedures
requests.request_text:
Provide documented incident response plan and related procedures used by the organization to respond immediately to a system breach, with defined processes as follows from 

-Create the incident response plan to be implemented in the event of a system breach.
-Test the plan at least annually.
-Designate specific personnel to be available on a 24/7 basis to respond to alerts:
- 24/7 incident monitoring
- 24/7 incident response
-Provide appropriate training to staff with security breach response responsibilities.
-Include alerts from security monitoring systems, including but not limited to intrusion-detection, intrusion-prevention, firewalls, and file integrity monitoring systems.
-  Develop a process to modify and evolve the incident response plan according to lessons learned and to incorporate industry developments.
(ROC|12.10.1)

requests.request_text:

requests.raw_requirement: Policy and procedures for Requirement 1
requests.request_text:
Purpose 
This Requirement is about effectively managing and maintaining the various policies and procedures specified throughout the Requirement. While it is important to define the specific policies or procedures called out in the requirement and sub-requirements, it is equally important to ensure they are properly documented, maintained, and disseminated. 

Definitions
Security policies define the entity’s security objectives and principles. Operational procedures describe how to perform activities, and define the controls, methods, and processes that are followed to achieve the desired result in a consistent manner and in accordance with policy objectives.

requests.request_text:
Purpose 
This Requirement is about effectively managing and maintaining the various policies and procedures specified throughout the Requirement. While it is important to define the specific policies or procedures called out in the requirement and sub-requirements, it is equally important to ensure they are properly documented, maintained, and disseminated. 

Definitions
Security policies define the entity’s security objectives and principles. Operational procedures describe how to perform activities, and define the controls, methods, and processes that are followed to achieve the desired result in a consistent manner and in accordance with policy objectives.

Policy and Procedure Documents should address all of the following topics:
-	Authorize and manage physical access of personnel to the CDE are defined
-	Authorizing and managing visitor access to the CDE
-	Protecting cardholder data include controls for physically securing all media
-	Physically securing offline media backups with cardholder data in a secure location.
-	Reviewing the security of the offline media backup location(s) with cardholder data at least once every 12 months
-	Storage location’s security is reviewed at least once every 12 months
-	Classifying media with cardholder data in accordance with the sensitivity of the data.
-	Securing media sent outside the facility
-	Ensure that media moved outside the facility is approved by management
-	Maintain electronic media inventory logs
-	Conduct inventories of electronic media with cardholder data at least once every 12 months
-	Destroy hard-copy media with cardholder data when no longer needed for business or legal reasons
-	Destroy electronic media when no longer needed for business or legal reasons 
-	Periodic inspections of POI device surfaces to detect tampering and unauthorized substitution
The documents must have been reviewed since the last assessment and contain updates or an entry in a document control table that shows the review or update. File dates and submit dates can not be used to validate this. This request may be fulfilled with one or multiple documents.

-	Processes are defined for addressing exceptions and anomalies identified during the review process
-	Audit Log Retention policies are defined

-	For Service Provider assessments, processes are defined for the prompt detection and addressing of failures of critical security control systems
-	Prompt detection and addressing of failures of critical security control systems
-	Processes are defined and implemented to respond to a failure of any critical security control  ystem

requests.raw_requirement: Policy and procedures for Requirement 11
requests.request_text:
Purpose 
This Requirement is about effectively managing and maintaining the various policies and procedures specified throughout the Requirement. While it is important to define the specific policies or procedures called out in the requirement and sub-requirements, it is equally important to ensure they are properly documented, maintained, and disseminated. 

Definitions
Security policies define the entity’s security objectives and principles. Operational procedures describe how to perform activities, and define the controls, methods, and processes that are followed to achieve the desired result in a consistent manner and in accordance with policy objectives.

Policy and procedure documentation should address the following topics:
-	Processes are defined for managing both authorized and unauthorized wireless access points
-	Processes are defined to detect and identify both authorized and unauthorized wireless access points 
-	Internal and External scan process includes rescans as needed to confirm the vulnerabilities have been addressed
-	Penetration-testing procedures are defined to test all segmentation methods
The documents must have been reviewed since the last assessment and contain updates or an entry in a document control table that shows the review or update. File dates and submit dates can not be used to validate this. The same document may be used for all requirements. One single document or multiple documents may be needed for this request.
(ROC|11.1.1, 11.2.1.a, 11.2.1.b, 11.3.1.1.b, 11.4.5.a, 11.4.5.a, 11.4.6.a, 11.4.7)

requests.raw_requirement: (Sample) Configuration settings for network security devices
requests.request_text:
Including: Firewalls, Routers, Switches, Load Balancers, Web Application Firewalls, Web Filters, and Web Proxy. Configuration exports or screenshots are acceptable but must detail the entire configuration. See instructions in the collection scripts folder of the PCI Assessment Resources in Project Files.
(ROC|1.2.2.b, 1.2.2.c, 6.5.3.b, 8.4.1.a)

requests.raw_requirement: Disclosure protections for IP addresses
requests.request_text:
Restricting the disclosure of internal, private, and local IP addresses is useful to prevent a hacker from obtaining knowledge of these IP addresses and using that information to access the network. 

Good Practice Methods used to meet the intent of this requirement may vary, depending on the specific networking technology being used. For example, the controls used to meet this requirement may be different for IPv4 networks than for IPv6 networks. 

Examples 
Methods to obscure IP addressing may include, but are not limited to: 
-IPv4 Network Address Translation (NAT). 
-Placing system components behind proxy servers/NSCs. 
-Removal or filtering of route advertisements for internal networks that use registered addressing. 
-Internal use of RFC 1918 (IPv4) or use IPv6 privacy extension (RFC 4941) when initiating outgoing sessions to the internet.
(ROC|1.4.5.b)

requests.raw_requirement: Prevent copying of PAN when remotely connected to the CDE
requests.request_text:
Examine documented policies and procedures and documented evidence for technical controls that prevent copy and/or relocation of PAN when using remote-access technologies onto local hard drives or removable electronic media to verify the following: 
- Technical controls prevent all personnel not specifically authorized from copying and/or relocating PAN. 
- A list of personnel with permission to copy and/or relocate PAN is maintained, together with the documented, explicit authorization and legitimate, defined business need.
(ROC|3.4.2.a)

requests.raw_requirement: Cryptographic suites and protocols documentation
requests.request_text:
The inventory of trusted keys helps the entity keep track of the algorithms, protocols, key strength, key custodians, and key expiry dates. This enables the entity to respond quickly to vulnerabilities discovered in encryption software, certificates, and cryptographic algorithms. For certificates, the inventory should include the issuing CA and certification expiration date.
(ROC|2.2.7.a, 2.3.1.c, 2.3.2, 3.3.2, 3.3.3.b, 3.5.1.b, 3.5.1.1.c, 3.5.1.2.a, 3.5.1.3.a, 3.6.1.1, 3.6.1.2.b, 4.2.1.b, 4.2.1.1.b, 4.2.1.2, 4.2.2.b, 8.3.2.b, 8.3.2.c, 8.6.2.b, 10.6.2, 12.3.3, 12.5.2.b, A3.2.1)

requests.raw_requirement: Inventory of payment page scripts
requests.request_text:
Scripts loaded and executed in the payment page can have their functionality altered without the entity’s knowledge and can also have the functionality to load additional external scripts (for example, advertising and tracking, tag management systems). 
Such seemingly harmless scripts can be used by potential attackers to upload malicious scripts that can read and exfiltrate cardholder data from the consumer browser. 
Ensuring that the functionality of all such scripts is understood to be necessary for the operation of the payment page minimizes the number of scripts that could be tampered with. 
Ensuring that scripts have been explicitly authorized reduces the probability of unnecessary scripts being added to the payment page without appropriate management approval. 
Using techniques to prevent tampering with the script will minimize the probability of the script being modified to carry out unauthorized behavior, such as skimming the cardholder data from the payment page.
(ROC|6.4.3.b)

requests.raw_requirement: Listing of accounts used by installed in-house or off-the-shelf applications
requests.request_text:
List of system and application accounts for all CDE systems. The assessor will use this information during interviews and observations to sample the use of default credentials.

requests.raw_requirement: Periodic reviews of user accounts
requests.request_text:
Regular review of access rights helps to detect excessive access rights remaining after user job responsibilities change, system functions change, or other modifications. If excessive user rights are not revoked in due time, they may be used by malicious users for unauthorized access. 

This review provides another opportunity to ensure that accounts for all terminated users have been removed (if any were missed at the time of termination), as well as to ensure that any third parties that no longer need access have had their access terminated.

When a user transfers into a new role or a new department, typically the privileges and access associated with their former role are no longer required. Continued access to privileges or functions that are no longer required may introduce the risk of misuse or errors. Therefore, when responsibilities change, processes that revalidate access help to ensure user access is appropriate for the user’s new responsibilities.

Entities can consider implementing a regular, repeatable process for conducting reviews of access rights, and assigning “data owners” that are responsible for managing and monitoring access to data related to their job function and that also ensure user access remains current and appropriate.
(ROC|7.2.4.b)

requests.raw_requirement: TRAs for the periodic review of application and system accounts and privileges
requests.request_text:
Targeted risk assessment results for the analysis of the need to perform review of access rights helps to detect excessive access rights remaining after system functions change, or other application or system modifications occur. If excessive rights are not removed when no longer needed, they may be used by malicious users for unauthorized access.
(ROC|7.2.5.1.b, 12.3.1)

requests.raw_requirement: Authorizations for account lifecycle maintenance
requests.request_text:
It is imperative that the lifecycle of a user ID (additions, deletions, and modifications) is controlled so that only authorized accounts can perform functions, actions are auditable, and privileges are limited to only what is required. Attackers often compromise an existing account and then escalate the privileges of that account to perform unauthorized acts, or they may create new IDs to continue their activity in the background. It is essential to detect and respond when user accounts are created or changed outside the normal change process or without corresponding authorization. Authorizations may be in the form of service requests, HRIS actions, Email, or other workflow management tools.
(ROC|8.2.4)

requests.raw_requirement: Deactivation validation for users whose employment has been terminated
requests.request_text:
If an employee or third party/vendor has left the company and still has access to the network via their user account, unnecessary or malicious access to cardholder data could occur—either by the former employee or by a malicious user who exploits the old and/or unused account. Evidence should show the deactivation in system user lists or that user lists no longer contain the user account for both local and remote access.
(ROC|8.2.5.a)

requests.raw_requirement: Third-party account access, status, and approvals
requests.request_text:
Documented approval (for example, in writing or electronically) that shows  authorization for  third-party access and privileges are known and authorized by management, and that their access is justified.
(ROC|8.2.7)

requests.raw_requirement: Packet captures of data transmissions from CDE systems
requests.request_text:
Packet captures that include the process of logging in to CDE systems to demonstrate that  devices and applications do not transmit unencrypted, readable authentication factors (such as passwords and passphrases) across the network.
(ROC|8.3.2.c)

requests.raw_requirement: Accounts used for both interactive login and for system or application access
requests.request_text:
Like individual user accounts, system and application accounts require accountability and strict management to ensure they are used only for the intended purpose and are not misused.
Attackers often compromise system or application accounts to gain access to cardholder data.
Where possible, configure system and application accounts to disallow interactive login to prevent unauthorized individuals from logging in and using the account with its associated system privileges, and to limit the machines and devices on which the account can be used.
System or application accounts are those accounts that execute processes or perform tasks on a computer system or application and are not typically accounts that an individual logs into. These accounts usually have elevated privileges that are required to perform specialized tasks or functions.
Interactive login is the ability for a person to log into a system or application account in the same manner as a normal user account. Using system and application accounts this way means there is no accountability and traceability of actions taken by the user.
(ROC|8.6.1, 11.3.1.2.c)

requests.raw_requirement: TRAs for change frequency and complexity of passwords or passphrases used for interactive login to application and system accounts
requests.request_text:
Provide targeted risk analysis results for the change frequency and complexity for passwords/passphrases used for interactive login to application and system accounts to verify the risk analysis was performed in accordance with all elements specified in Requirement 12.3.1 and addresses:
-The frequency defined for periodic changes to application and system passwords/passphrases.
-The complexity defined for passwords/passphrases and appropriateness of the complexity relative to the frequency of changes.
(ROC|8.6.3.b, 12.3.1)

requests.raw_requirement: Annual hardware and software review
requests.request_text:
Hardware and software technologies in use are reviewed at least once every 12 months, including at least the following:

-Documentation of any industry announcements or trends related to a technology, such as when a vendor has announced “end of life” plans for a technology.

Reviews are performed by personnel other than those responsible for performing the given task and include, but are not limited to, the following tasks: 
-Daily log reviews. 
-Configuration reviews for network security controls. 
-Applying configuration standards to new systems. 
-Responding to security alerts. 
-Change management processes.

requests.raw_requirement: Annual scope reviews
requests.request_text:
PCI DSS scope is documented and confirmed by the entity at least once every 12 months and upon significant change to the in-scope environment. At a minimum, the scoping validation includes: 
-Identifying all data flows for the various payment stages (for example, authorization, capture settlement, chargebacks, and refunds) and acceptance channels (for example, card-present, card-not-present, and e-commerce). 
-Updating all data-flow diagrams per Requirement 1.2.4. 
-Identifying all locations where account data is stored, processed, and transmitted, including but not limited to: 1) any locations outside of the currently defined CDE, 2) applications that process CHD, 3) transmissions between systems and networks, and 4) file backups. 
-Identifying all system components in the CDE, connected to the CDE, or that could impact security of the CDE. 
-Identifying all segmentation controls in use and the environment(s) from which the CDE is segmented, including justification for environments being out of scope. 
-Identifying all connections from third-party entities with access to the CDE. 
-Confirming that all identified data flows, account data, system components, segmentation controls, and connections from third parties with access to the CDE are included in scope.
(ROC|ES, 12.5.2.a, 12.5.2.b)

requests.raw_requirement: Policy and procedures for evaluating scope after a significant change
requests.request_text:
Significant changes to organizational structure result in a documented (internal) review of the impact to PCI DSS scope and applicability of controls, with results communicated to executive management.

Provide policies and procedures to verify that processes are defined such that a significant change to organizational structure results in documented review of the impact to PCI DSS scope and applicability of controls.

requests.raw_requirement: Scope evaluations and executive communications after significant changes
requests.request_text:
Significant changes to organizational structure result in a documented (internal) review of the impact to PCI DSS scope and applicability of controls, with results communicated to executive management.

Provide formal internal documentation that shows scope was evaluated after a significant change and the results were communicated to executive management.

requests.raw_requirement: Security awareness training content
requests.request_text:
The threat environment and an entity’s defenses are not static. As such, the security awareness program materials must be updated as frequently as needed to ensure that the education received by personnel is up to date and represents the current threat environment.

An effective security awareness program should include examples of phishing emails and periodic testing to determine the prevalence of personnel reporting such attacks. Training material an entity can consider for this topic include: 
- How to identify phishing and other social engineering attacks. 
- How to react to suspected phishing and social engineering. 
- Where and how to report suspected phishing and social engineering activity.

Different methods that can be used to provide security awareness and education include posters, letters, web-based training, in-person training, team meetings, and incentives. Personnel acknowledgments may be recorded in writing or electronically.
(ROC|12.6.2, 12.6.3.b, 12.6.3.d, 12.6.3.d, 12.6.3.1, 12.6.3.2)

requests.raw_requirement: Agreements with third-party service providers
requests.request_text:
Provide copies of written agreements with Third-Party Service Providers that show: 
-Written agreements are maintained with all TPSPs with which account data is shared or that could affect the security of the CDE. 
-Written agreements include acknowledgments from TPSPs that they are responsible for the security of account data the TPSPs possess or otherwise store, process, or transmit on behalf of the entity, or to the extent that they could impact the security of the entity’s CDE.
(ROC|12.8.2.b)

requests.raw_requirement: Vendor engagement processes
requests.request_text:
Provide formal internal documentation that shows an established process is implemented for engaging TPSPs, including proper due diligence prior to engagement.

Specific due-diligence processes and goals will vary for each organization. Elements that should be considered include the provider’s reporting practices, breach-notification and incident response procedures, details of how PCI DSS responsibilities are assigned between each party, how the TPSP validates their PCI DSS compliance and what evidence they provide.
(ROC|12.8.3.b)

requests.raw_requirement: Responsibilities are managed by each TPSP and shared with the entity
requests.request_text:
Information is maintained about which PCI DSS requirements are managed by each TPSP, which are managed by the entity, and any that are shared between the TPSP and the entity.

Entities can document these responsibilities via a matrix that identifies all applicable PCI DSS requirements and indicates for each requirement whether the entity or TPSP is responsible for meeting that requirement or whether it is a shared responsibility. This type of document is often referred to as a responsibility matrix.

It is also important for entities to understand whether any TPSPs have “nested” relationships with other TPSPs, meaning the primary TPSP contracts with another TPSP(s) for the purposes of providing a service. It is important to understand whether the primary TPSP is relying on the secondary TPSP(s) to achieve overall compliance of a service, and how the primary TPSP is monitoring performance of the service and the PCI DSS compliance status of the secondary TPSP(s). Note that it is the responsibility of the primary TPSP to manage and monitor any secondary TPSPs.

Refer to Information Supplement: Third-Party Security Assurance for a sample responsibility matrix template.
(ROC|12.8.5.b)

requests.raw_requirement: TPSP PCI DSS responsibilities

Security policies define the entity’s security objectives and principles. Operational procedures describe how to perform activities, and define the controls, methods, and processes that are followed to achieve the desired result in a consistent manner and in accordance with policy objectives.
